Additional snow chances continued mid-week, mostly for the northern Sierra. Conditions continued to be treacherous over the Sierra overnight on the 4th with breezy winds, snow, and blowing snow. Conditions briefly improved over the Sierra the morning through the afternoon of the 5th with additional snow shower chances continuing thereafter, especially for the Sierra north of US-50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 5.0 mi WSW of June Lake Mountain Ski resort reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 10.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, very strong, winter storm impacted Southwestern California. Rainfall totals were very significant across the area. Los Angeles county reported rain totals of 4 to 14 inches, Ventura county reported 2 to 10 inches, Santa Barbara county reported 4 to 12 inches and San Luis Obispo county reported 2 to 5 inches. With the heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flooding were received. In the mountains, very significant snowfall was reported with amounts in the 1 to 3 foot range. Also, severe thunderstorms and tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported significant flooding on Highway 101 near Campo de Cahuenga. Numerous vehicles were stuck in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season, storm brought significant rainfall to the area along with strong thunderstorms. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties to 1 to 2 inches across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Unstable air, associated with the storm, brought scattered strong thunderstorms to the area, producing gusty winds, large hail and even some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in the Monrovia area, generated debris flows in the area around the Fish Fire burn scar. Per local emergency managers, the debris catch basins were over halfway full after the debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, late season, storm rolled across Southwestern California. This storm brought strong north to northeast winds as well as strong thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds  and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the eastern range of the Santa Ynez Mountains. The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported peak gusts of 69 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving cold front came through California on January 31, bringing widespread rain and high winds. A narrow cold frontal rainband (NCFR) developed during the afternoon and evening commute, with moderate to heavy rain across the region as a result. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough that dug across the interior West Saturday and Sunday, |aligned with the left exit region of a meridional jet favoring large scale ascent across the Sierra and western Nevada. This setup for strong winds and widespread precipitation across the region. This included the potential for heavy Sierra snowfall with light snow accumulation on valley floors. As the first snow arrived Saturday morning wind observations over Sierra ridges showed gusts ranging 70-80 mph. This system was considered the coldest and most impactful storm up to this point this season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 5.0 mi WNW of Susanville, CA, reported a 24 hour storm total snowfall amount of 7.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger and colder more classic winter storm followed Wednesday through|early Thursday, bringing additional wind and snow impacts to the Sierra and western NV. This storm system���s trajectory allowed it to drop southward into eastern California and western Nevada Wednesday-Wednesday night. This storm started with a short round of strong winds followed by bursts of heavier snow. Although this storm appeared to have a more overland trajectory with liquid precipitation totals a little lower, the forcing from this low and attendant cold front remained sufficiently intact to produce additional snow with at least an even chance of 6 or more inches in the Tahoe basin southward to western Mono County, and an additional foot or more along the Sierra crest. Plenty of winter travel hazards at all elevations continued into the Thursday AM commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Heavenly Ski Base, CA, the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, CA, and the Northstar Ski Base, CA, reported 24 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 21.0 inches, 14.0 inches and 9.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was allowed to tap into Pacific moisture for a longer period of time that nudged it just enough north to make a more direct landfall into the central OR coast.  This next storm Friday evening into Saturday brought more gusty winds, Sierra snow, and valley rain/snow showers. This storm���s warmer nature raised snow levels a bit higher(5500-6500 feet) with the potential for mixed precipitation in the valley areas and foothills. The lower-end ensemble snow scenarios showed a bit more confident projection for at least a foot of snow in the Sierra with potential for 18-24 inches along the Sierra crest. Regardless, this contributed to another bout of travel disruptions, especially for ski commutes during the upcoming holiday weekend. Wind speeds have trended up a bit with this storm as well with the potential for wind gusts 40 to 50 mph for the valley areas. Sierra ridge wind gusts got a boost with potential for gusts 90+ mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (DYLC1-Doyle) located 3.0 mi N of Doyle, CA,  reported a peak wind gust 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warmer storm brought more rain, high elevation snow, and gusty southwesterly winds late Tuesday into Wednesday. An upper-level low moving into the Pacific Northwest flattened a persistent ridge on Tuesday afternoon, and allowed warmer subtropical moisture to be transported into our region with the increased more zonally directed westerly flow aloft. Higher ridge level winds increased late Tuesday morning as an upper jet oriented over the region bringing peak overnight wind gusts to around 80-100 mph. Ridge winds remained elevated through Wednesday, with breezy SW winds mixing down to valleys by Wednesday afternoon. The main precipitation band moved into the region by Tuesday PM wrapping up by late Wednesday AM. Snow levels remained a bit higher than previous storms due to the subtropical tap. Snow levels will hovered between 8000-8500 feet during the main precipitation event, briefly topping upwards to 9000 feet at times. Snow accumulations were confined to the high Sierra, with predicted storm totals ranging 2-6 inches above 8000 feet. In terms of liquid totals, the highest values expected stayed along the Sierra crest, generally between 0.75-1 inches. A sharp gradient in liquid precipitation persisted east of the crest due to stronger rain shadowing with 0.25-0.5 inches generally west of US-395 and trace-0.1 inch east of Highway 395.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MPRJN - Mid Mtn Chalet.) located 1.0 mi SE of June Mountain, CA, and mesonet station  (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA,  reported peak wind gusts of 127 mph and 96 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather returns Wednesday afternoon to close out the month of January. Therefore, strong and gusty winds especially over higher mountain elevations was followed with mountain snow and valley rain showers. Wind was the first hazard expected starting over the Sierra and NE CA, then spreading into western Nevada with south winds between 30-50 mph, and gusts over 60-80 mph across higher mountain ridges and passes. Valley winds were projected to be 15-35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The probability of 60 mph winds across mountain areas in CA was 60% or greater and 10-20% over W NV. Lake winds were around 15-25 mph with gusts to 35-45 mph.||Valley rain and mountain snow showers were projected to move into the region   Wednesday PM. Global models and their respective ensemble members initially dug the trough and a deeper layer of sub-tropical IVT moisture will quickly sweep east and south across portions of northeast California, before descending into the Sierra, western Nevada, and Mono Co. Snow levels started near 7000 feet on Wednesday, then dropped to 6000 feet by Thursday. Most higher elevation areas received mostly rain initially before transitioning to mostly snow Thursday evening. Snow levels dropped to 5500 ft on Thursday afternoon, and dropped further to below 4000 feet by Friday morning. The Sierra above 7000 and Mono Co were given even chances for about 1-2 feet of new snow with 6-12 inches below 7000 feet. For northeast California 4-10 inches of accumulated snow, and up to 20 inches in the higher mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH22-MAMMOTH) located 1.0 mi ESE of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 99 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on January 6 then dropped southeast into the Great Basin by the morning of January 7, A cold front that accompanied this system crossed central California during the evening of January 6 bringing a period of light to moderate precipitation and strong winds to the area. Several stations in the mountains and deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations exceeding 70 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

The fog lifted into a stratus deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved through central California during the early morning of January 17 producing widespread light precipitation in the area. Skies cleared out behind this system and allowed for areas of dense fog to form near daybreak in the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley and in the Tehachapi area. The fog lifted by late morning except in the far south end of the valley and around Tehachapi where the fog hung around into the afternoon before lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure remained over central California on January 29 bringing near record high  temperatures to much of our area. Clear skies and light winds during the early morning resulted in areas of dense fog forming in the San Joaquin Valley. The fog reduced visibility to a few hundred feet in some locations and was responsible for a two vehicle accident in southeast Fresno which resulted in two fatalities. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano. Web cameras indicated visibility below 500 feet.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on January 6 then dropped southeast into the Great Basin by the morning of January 7, A cold front that accompanied this system crossed central California during the evening of January 6 bringing a period of light to moderate precipitation and strong winds to the area. Several stations in the mountains and deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations exceeding 70 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wet Meadows SNOTEL (9019 feet) picked up an estimated 14 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell through the overnight hours on January 7, resulting in 2-4 inches of accumulations in the San Bernardino County Mountains. Chain controls were in effect for Highway 2, Highway 243, Highway 18, Highway 330, Highway 38 and Highway 189.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active month continued on March 23-24, as another storm system brought more precipitation impacts to Southern California. The main impacts were in the form of snow at some mountain communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass on March 23rd. Peak wind gusts were generally in the 40-55 mph range. Some notable higher gusts include 63 mph at the Whitewater RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure entered the western US on March 13th. This allowed for a strong round of Santa Ana Winds to impact Southern California on March 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong Santa Ana winds impacted coastal portions of Orange County on March 13th and 14th. Peak wind gusts were generally in the 20-40 mph range. Some notable higher gusts include 59 mph at Huntington Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system dropped southeast over the Great Basin on January 3 which pushed a cold front across central California. This system produced moderate to locally heavy rainfall for a short time over the lower elevations and 12 to 18 inches of new snowfall above 6000 feet from Fresno County northward as well as 5 to 10  inches of next snow above 6000 feet in Tulare County. Rainfall amounts were generally a half inch to an inch of rain in the Sierra foothills, a quarter to half inch of rainfall in the central San Joaquin Valley and a tenth to a quarter inch in the south San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved into central California on January 17 which resulted in clearing skies and light winds over the area by the evening. With low level moisture present in the San Joaquin Valley due to recent rainfall, dense fog formed overnight and continued through the late morning hours of January 18 until it lifted into a low cloud deck. The fog was responsible for a 6 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which resulted in one fatality and several injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure strengthened off the California coast overnight and pushed inland during the early morning of January 26. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Web cameras indicated visibility below 500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mixing ahead of an incoming trough resulted in mainly clear skies across the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 10. With light winds also prevailing over the lower elevations, areas of dense fog formed near sunrise in portions of the San Joaquin Valley, most noticeably along the SR 99 corridor north of Fresno where travel was impacted by visibilities below 500 feet in places. The fog prevailed through late morning before mixing  out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening high pressure over central California brought clearing skies and light winds to the area during the morning of January 28. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough moved across central California during the evening of January 10 and the early morning of January 11. This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. Areas further south in the Sierra Nevada and much of the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter and a half inch while much of the San Joaquin Valley received between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts with several stations in the Tehachapi area and the Mojave Deserts slopes measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season, storm brought significant rainfall to the area along with strong thunderstorms. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties to 1 to 2 inches across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Unstable air, associated with the storm, brought scattered strong thunderstorms to the area, producing gusty winds, large hail and even some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms generated heavy rainfall in the Monrovia area. This heavy rain generated flash flooding. Several business on Huntington Drive between Santa Anita Avenue and 2nd Avenue reported significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system produced snow in the mountains and light rain across the coast and valleys. Several inches of snow were reported in the mountains, and thundersnow occurred in the Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the San Bernardino County Mountains on January 3, with accumulations as low as 4900 feet reported. Most areas saw 1-3 inches, with locations near Snow Summit, Snow Valley, Green Valley Lake and Forest Falls seeing up to 4 inches in accumulations. Numerous vehicle incidents were reported in the mountains as a result of snow covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system produced snow in the mountains and light rain across the coast and valleys. Several inches of snow were reported in the mountains, and thundersnow occurred in the Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the Riverside County Mountains on January 3, with accumulations as low as 5400 feet reported. Most areas saw 1-3 inches, with Long Valley Mount San Jacinto seeing up to 6 inches of snow. Thundersnow was reported near Mount San Jacinto 4-6pm on January 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system produced snow in the mountains and light rain across the coast and valleys. Several inches of snow were reported in the mountains, and thundersnow occurred in the Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1 inch of snow fell as low as 5700 feet in the San Diego County Mountains on January 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mixing took place ahead of an incoming storm during the early morning of January 3 allowing for skies to clear out and some patches of dense fog to briefly form and impact travel along portions of SR 99. The fog mixed out by late morning as the storm moved into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system dropped southeast over the Great Basin on January 3 which pushed a cold front across central California. This system produced moderate to locally heavy rainfall for a short time over the lower elevations and 12 to 18 inches of new snowfall above 6000 feet from Fresno County northward as well as 5 to 10  inches of next snow above 6000 feet in Tulare County. Rainfall amounts were generally a half inch to an inch of rain in the Sierra foothills, a quarter to half inch of rainfall in the central San Joaquin Valley and a tenth to a quarter inch in the south San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported debris on southbound Interstate 5 at the Grapevine Commercial Vehicle Enforcement Facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mixing ahead of an incoming trough resulted in mainly clear skies across the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 10. With light winds also prevailing over the lower elevations, areas of dense fog formed near sunrise in portions of the San Joaquin Valley, most noticeably along the SR 99 corridor north of Fresno where travel was impacted by visibilities below 500 feet in places. The fog prevailed through late morning before mixing  out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on January 6 then dropped southeast into the Great Basin by the morning of January 7, A cold front that accompanied this system crossed central California during the evening of January 6 bringing a period of light to moderate precipitation and strong winds to the area. Several stations in the mountains and deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations exceeding 70 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tehachapi East Valley measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough moved across central California during the evening of January 10 and the early morning of January 11. This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. Areas further south in the Sierra Nevada and much of the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter and a half inch while much of the San Joaquin Valley received between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts with several stations in the Tehachapi area and the Mojave Deserts slopes measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poison Ridge SNOTEL (9688 feet) picked up an estimated 12 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over the San Joaquin Valley behind the winter storm which moved to the east of the area on January 11. With clear skies, light winds and inversion conditions, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning on January 12. Increasing clouds spread southward into the valley during the morning of January 13 which kept minimum temperatures above the freezing mark across the central San Joaquin Valley. Portions of the south valley dropped below the freezing mark again during the morning of January 13 as skies remained mainly clear until late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 27 and 31 degrees on January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved into central California on January 17 which resulted in clearing skies and light winds over the area by the evening. With low level moisture present in the San Joaquin Valley due to recent rainfall, dense fog formed overnight and continued through the late morning hours of January 18 until it lifted into a low cloud deck. The fog was responsible for a 6 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which resulted in one fatality and several injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet. A 6 vehicle accident with three tractor trailers and three cars occurred at 557 PST on Westbound SR 152 at Road 9. The accident resulted in one fatality and several serious injuries. Visibility was below 1000 feet during the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The co-op station 4SSW Ridgecrest measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This comparatively weaker winter storm opened up another active weather pattern that tracked through the Sierra and W.Nevada late in the evening February 17-18. This storm brought higher elevation snowfall that was heavy at times above 7000 feet. This storm also included the possibility of an additional several feet of snowfall.  Mainly spillover rainfall fell over lower western Nevada valleys that included periods of gusty winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03-MAMMOTH) located 1.7 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 112 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional snow chances continued mid-week, mostly for the northern Sierra. Conditions continued to be treacherous over the Sierra overnight on the 4th with breezy winds, snow, and blowing snow. Conditions briefly improved over the Sierra the morning through the afternoon of the 5th with additional snow shower chances continuing thereafter, especially for the Sierra north of US-50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH15-Chair 9 Tower 11) located 1.0 mi E of Mammoth Mountain Ski resort reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This first of two impactful weather systems arrived in the area during afternoon on the 27th with the passage of a cold front. Gusty to strong winds across the region as well as valley rain and mountain snow accompanied this initial storm system through the morning of the 28th. Following a brief, a second impactful system brought additional precipitation chances for mountain snow and valley rain that started overnight on the 29th that continued through the Easter weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Homewood Mountain Ski Base, CA, and 4.0 mi NW of Truckee, CA, reported 24 hour snowfall amounts of 10.0 inches and 6.0 inches respectively.

Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CWOP station at South Ridgecrest measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough moved across central California during the evening of January 10 and the early morning of January 11. This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved through the Pacific Northwest on January 13 brought precipitation as far south as central California between the early morning of January 13 and the morning of January 14. While precipitation amounts were fairly light, the system did produce 4 to 8 inches of snow above 7000 feet in the Yosemite Park area. Several stations further south picked up an estimated 1 to 4 inches of snowfall above 7000 feet. Rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less in the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills and a tenth to a quarter inch in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog formed in the south San Joaquin Valley and the valley facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of January 15 lowering visibility in some locations to be low a quarter mile. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed a funnel cloud in northeast Madera.

CZ_NAME: NORTHWESTERN LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS INCLUDING THE INTERSTATE 5 CORRIDOR
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, late season, storm rolled across Southwestern California. This storm brought strong north to northeast winds as well as strong thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds  and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the Interstate 5 Corridor in the Northwest Los Angeles County Mountains. The Sandberg ASOS reported gusts up to 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, late season, storm rolled across Southwestern California. This storm brought strong north to northeast winds as well as strong thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds  and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the western range of the Santa Monica Mountains. Some peak gusts include: Deer Creek Canyon (gust 68 MPH), Rancho Del Cielo (gust 59 MPH) and Corral Canyon (gust 58 MPH).

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY SAN GABRIEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season, storm brought significant rainfall to the area along with strong thunderstorms. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties to 1 to 2 inches across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Unstable air, associated with the storm, brought scattered strong thunderstorms to the area, producing gusty winds, large hail and even some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in the Monrovia area reported thunderstorm winds with estimated gusts of 50 MPH. The winds knocked down numerous tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm system produced strong winds over the mountains and desert slopes as well as mountain snow on January 7. Chain controls were in effect across the San Bernardino County Mountains through the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass near Banning from the evening of January 6 through the morning on January 7. Widespread wind gusts over 35 mph were reported in this area, with a number of gusts over 50 knots. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out overnight across the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning of January 1. This allowed for areas of dense fog to form by sunrise as winds remined light. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed in the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains by late evening on January 1. The dense fog became more prevalent across the San Joaquin  Valley overnight and remained persistent in the south end of the valley until late morning on January 2. The fog also contributed to a serious accident on SR 99 during the late morning south of  Bakersfield which injured a motorist. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Bakersfield. California Highway Patrol reported a vehicle overturned on northbound SR 99 near The Union Ave. exchange south of Bakersfield at 1030 PST in visibility below a quarter of a mile. The driver of the vehicle was injured in the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out over the San Joaquin valley during the evening of January 18 and areas of dense of formed overnight during the early morning of January 19. The fog dissipated by late morning as mixing took place ahead of an incoming storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved through central California during the early morning of January 17 producing widespread light precipitation in the area. Skies cleared out behind this system and allowed for areas of dense fog to form near daybreak in the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley and in the Tehachapi area. The fog lifted by late morning except in the far south end of the valley and around Tehachapi where the fog hung around into the afternoon before lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Tehachapi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved into central California on January 17 which resulted in clearing skies and light winds over the area by the evening. With low level moisture present in the San Joaquin Valley due to recent rainfall, dense fog formed overnight and continued through the late morning hours of January 18 until it lifted into a low cloud deck. The fog was responsible for a 6 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which resulted in one fatality and several injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough moved across central California during the evening of January 10 and the early morning of January 11. This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. Areas further south in the Sierra Nevada and much of the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter and a half inch while much of the San Joaquin Valley received between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts with several stations in the Tehachapi area and the Mojave Deserts slopes measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over the San Joaquin Valley behind the winter storm which moved to the east of the area on January 11. With clear skies, light winds and inversion conditions, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning on January 12. Increasing clouds spread southward into the valley during the morning of January 13 which kept minimum temperatures above the freezing mark across the central San Joaquin Valley. Portions of the south valley dropped below the freezing mark again during the morning of January 13 as skies remained mainly clear until late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 27 and 31 degrees on January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure remained over central California on January 29 bringing near record high  temperatures to much of our area. Clear skies and light winds during the early morning resulted in areas of dense fog forming in the San Joaquin Valley. The fog reduced visibility to a few hundred feet in some locations and was responsible for a two vehicle accident in southeast Fresno which resulted in two fatalities. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in west Fresno. Web cameras indicated visibility below 500 feet. At 743 PST a two vehicle accident occurred at the intersection of Chestnut Ave. and Annadale Ave. in southeast Fresno involving a pickup truck and a semi-truck. There were two fatalities as a result of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog formed in the south San Joaquin Valley and the valley facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of January 15 lowering visibility in some locations to be low a quarter mile. Enough mixing took place during the early morning of January 16 for the fog to lift into a low cloud deck which minimized travel impacts by the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Tehachapi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved across northern California during the evening of January 16 and the early morning of January 17 brought some light precipitation to the area with much of the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level near 7000 feet. Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 3 to 6 inches of new snowfall. Most of the central San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain while the south valley and the Kern County Mountains and Deserts generally had a tenth of an inch or less. This system also produced a brief period of strong wind gusts in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few locations measuring gusts exceeding 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Paradise Cove measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station on Grapevine Canyon Rd. measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm system produced strong winds over the mountains and desert slopes as well as mountain snow on January 7. Chain controls were in effect across the San Bernardino County Mountains through the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the High Desert from the evening of January 6 through the afternoon on January 7. Widespread wind gusts over 35 mph were reported in this area, with a number of gusts over 50 knots. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm system produced strong winds over the mountains and desert slopes as well as mountain snow on January 7. Chain controls were in effect across the San Bernardino County Mountains through the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell through the overnight hours on January 7, resulting in 2-4 inches of accumulations in the San Bernardino County Mountains. Chain controls were in effect for Highway 2, Highway 243, Highway 18, Highway 330, Highway 38 and Highway 189.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system produced strong winds across much of Southern California January 10-11, with the highest winds recorded in the mountains and along the desert slopes the morning of January 11. Scattered showers occurred mainly across San Diego County, and several inches of snow were recorded in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass near Banning from the evening of January 10 through the late morning hours on January 11. The strongest winds occurred from early to mid-morning on January 11. The following are the strongest winds recorded across the zone:||Whitewater (SCE1015): 80 MPH (1681')|Whitewater RAWS: 70 MPH (2545')|Desert Hot Springs (SCE566): 64 MPH (1518')|Whitewater (SCE1289): 62 MPH (2192')|Snowcreek Cyn (SCE577): 62 MPH (1180')|Whitewater Cyn (SCE581): 62 MPH (1403')|Whitewater South: 60 MPH (1133')|Banning (SCE1645): 58 MPH (2442').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Tyndall Creek SNOTEL (11443 feet) picked up an estimated 12 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pampa Peak measured a peak wind gust of76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak storm system moved across central California between the afternoon of February 17 and the early morning of February 18. Skies cleared out behind this system as it moved east of the area, and with abundant low level moisture, clearing skies and light winds, areas of dense fog formed in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley near daybreak and resulted in travel impacts along interstate 5 as well as along portions of SR 99 and SR 58 in the Bakersfield area. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Merced Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system produced strong winds across much of Southern California January 10-11, with the highest winds recorded in the mountains and along the desert slopes the morning of January 11. Scattered showers occurred mainly across San Diego County, and several inches of snow were recorded in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the High Desert from the afternoon of January 10 through the late morning hours on January 11. The strongest winds occurred from early to mid-morning on January 11. The following are the strongest winds recorded across the zone:||Lucerne Valley (SCE266): 65 MPH (3938')|Cushenbury Springs (SCE269): 65 MPH (4099')|Lucerne Valley (SCE262): 61 MPH (3946')|Lucerne Valley (SCE484): 61 MPH (4490')|Johnson Valley (SCE400): 60 MPH (3107').

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid week, after a rather tranquil start to the New Year, a weak occluded low split from the broader trough, then elongated as it drove south west of the Sierra crest limiting QPF totals over our region. Guidance showed that frontogenetic forcing, however, would amplify in central Nevada, and stretch west into the eastern Sierra and Mono County. The enhanced lift, coupled with precipitation banding, as projected by EC EFI/SOT and blended guidance showed a snowfall ���Boom��� potential  for at least moderate snow south of US-50 in western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP Observer (TAAC1) located in Tahoma, CA, and a CoCoRaHS station (CA-ED-9) located 0.5 mi S of Tahoma, CA both reported a 24 hour snowfall total of 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over the eastern Pacific Ocean, and strong high pressure well inland resulted in a tight gradient with south to southwest winds reaching warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deer Mountain reported peak winds of 35 mph with gusts to 70 mph. Van Bremmer reported peak winds of 26 mph with gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that a member of the public was unable to access his vehicle due to roadway flooding in Winton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary associated with an upper trough pushed through central California between the afternoon of February 20 and the morning of February 21. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the boundary and many locations in the San Joaquin Valley picked up an additional quarter to half inch of rainfall as a result. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up an additional half inch to an inch of additional liquid precipitation with several SNOTEL stations north of Kings Canyon picking up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. The frontal boundary stalled over Kern County during the early morning of February 21 resulting in nuisance flooding along several highways. The showers ended by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the offramp flooded on SR 99 at the Copus Rd. exchange.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low approached the northern California coast on March 22. The associated trough with this system pushed into central California by the evening bringing widespread precipitation to the area. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between 0.75 inches and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation while several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 10 to 15 inches of new snowfall. Most of the precipitation occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 23. Moderate rainfall in the Fresno area resulted in a fatal accident on SR 99 near the intersection of Clovis Ave. This system also brought a period of gusty winds to the Mojave Desert slopes between the evening of March 23 and the morning of March 24 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paradise Meadow SNOTEL (7676 feet) picked up an estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of winter storms that was directed a bit more south of Lake Tahoe along the eastern Sierra into Mono county. This system brought additional snow amounts to much of the Sierra late Sunday into Monday with periods of lower elevation/valley rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds. The main area of focus shifted from the Tahoe basin to Mono county where the White Mountains looked to get 2-3 inches of new snow while Mammoth Mountain was projected to receive another 5 to 10 inches of fresh snow. Snow levels hovered around 7000 feet for much of this second winter storm, then lowered to near 6000 feet towards the tail end of the storm late Monday. ECMWF EFI signals for CAPE across western Nevada showed higher instability with this second storm that indicated a better spillover potential and for showers to  persist well into Monday afternoon and evening. Storm liquid precipitation totals with this storm was projected to be about 1.50 inches to 2.00 inches along the span of the Sierra crest that translated to another 1 to 2 plus feet of snowfall for elevations above 8000 feet with this second storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the June Lake Ski Base, and Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA, reported 48 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 11.0 inches and 10.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Valley Wildfire was discovered on May 21st at 1231 PM PST. The fire is located 21 miles southeast of Alturas, in the Jess Valley of the Modoc National Forest. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave ridging prevailed over central California during the morning of February 3. With clear skies and light winds over the San Joaquin Valley, areas of dense fog formed along the SR 198 corridor between Visalia and Hanford toward daybreak locally reducing visibility to below 500 feet. The fog completely dissipated later in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave ridging prevailed over central California during the morning of February 3. With clear skies and light winds over the San Joaquin Valley, areas of dense fog formed along the SR 198 corridor between Visalia and Hanford toward daybreak locally reducing visibility to below 500 feet. The fog completely dissipated later in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported large boulders in all lanes of SR 178 near the mouth of the Kern River Canyon.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pascoes SNOTEL (9153 feet) picked up an estimated 22 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough that dug across the interior West Saturday and Sunday, |aligned with the left exit region of a meridional jet favoring large scale ascent across the Sierra and western Nevada. This setup for strong winds and widespread precipitation across the region. This included the potential for heavy Sierra snowfall with light snow accumulation on valley floors. As the first snow arrived Saturday morning wind observations over Sierra ridges showed gusts ranging 70-80 mph. This system was considered the coldest and most impactful storm up to this point this season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski main lodge reported storm total snowfall of 9.0 inches at the main lodge, and 11.0 inches at the summit height.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary associated with an upper trough pushed through central California between the afternoon of February 20 and the morning of February 21. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the boundary and many locations in the San Joaquin Valley picked up an additional quarter to half inch of rainfall as a result. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up an additional half inch to an inch of additional liquid precipitation with several SNOTEL stations north of Kings Canyon picking up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. The frontal boundary stalled over Kern County during the early morning of February 21 resulting in nuisance flooding along several highways. The showers ended by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A truck slid off of SR 99 and crashed into a divider near the exchange with Ave 200. The driver of the truck was killed in the accident. A flood watch was in effect for Tulare at the time of the accident and rain was ongoing as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper trough that brought unsettled weather conditions to central California for several days moved east of the area on February 9 while high pressure strengthened off the coast and began building inland during the morning of February 10. Some patches of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of February 10 where skies had cleared out while visibility remained good in other areas where clouds persisted. Web cameras showed low visibility along SR 99 between Madera and Chowchilla for a few hours. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera. Web cameras indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Madera and Chowchilla.

Several stations in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up an additional half inch to an inch of additional liquid precipitation with several SNOTEL stations north of Kings Canyon picking up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. The frontal boundary stalled over Kern County during the early morning of February 21 resulting in nuisance flooding along several highways. The showers ended by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a semi truck flipped over on its side on I-5 5 N Grapevine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Grapevine Rd. East measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY SAN GABRIEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, late season, storm rolled across Southwestern California. This storm brought strong north to northeast winds as well as strong thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds  and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the San Gabriel Valley. Some peak gusts include: Altadena (gust 69 MPH), San Dimas (gust 61 MPH) and Eaton Canyon (gust 60 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding near the intersection of Fields Rd. and La Grange Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Northstar Ski Base both reported 48 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 8.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most powerful storm of March brought significant rain, mountain snow, strong thunderstorms and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 1.50 to 4.00 inches across coastal and valley areas and up to 7 inches across the mountain slopes. At higher elevations, over 1 foot of snowfall was reported across the Ventura and Los Angeles county mountains. Strong thunderstorms generated gusty outflow winds and heavy rain which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large boulder across Highway 154 near Painted Cave Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the eastern range of the Santa Monica Mountains. Some peak gusts include: Saddle Peak (gust 67 MPH), Backbone Trail (gust 66 MPH) and Malibu Hills RAWS (gust 65 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early April system brought moderate to heavy snow to the mountains and upper foothills of interior northern California from April 3rd through April 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS near Shingletown measured snow amounts of 8.8 inches at 3980 feet, and 10 inches at 3630 feet over 24 hours. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 2 to 8 inches over 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early April system brought moderate to heavy snow to the mountains and upper foothills of interior northern California from April 3rd through April 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS near Grass Valley measured 3 inches of snow in 24 hours at 2782 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated storm total snow amounts of 1 to 6 inches over 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early April system brought moderate to heavy snow to the mountains and upper foothills of interior northern California from April 3rd through April 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple CoCoRaHS observers measured heavy snow, with 6 inches near Volcano at 2426 feet, 6 inches near Camino at 3038 feet, and 6.6 inches near Sonora at 2538 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing estimated 2 to 12 inches of snow over 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Grapevine measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wet Meadows SNOTEL (9019 feet) picked up and estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a power pole landed on a car and injured an occupant 5NNE Dos Palos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most powerful storm of March brought significant rain, mountain snow, strong thunderstorms and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 1.50 to 4.00 inches across coastal and valley areas and up to 7 inches across the mountain slopes. At higher elevations, over 1 foot of snowfall was reported across the Ventura and Los Angeles county mountains. Strong thunderstorms generated gusty outflow winds and heavy rain which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding on Highway 101 at Sheffield. Per officer reports, six inches of water was observed across the southbound lanes of Highway 101.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered over the Desert Southwest provided for a warm and dry airmass over the area. With plenty of residual low level moisture, light wind and clearing skies, an area of dense fog formed during the morning of February 24 along the SR 99 corridor between Kingsburg and Tulare. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 as well as along SR 198 through Visalia where webcams indicated visibility was below 1000 feet. The fog mixed out by 830 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Visalia. Several web cameras indicated visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Stallion Springs Bold Venture measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most powerful storm of March brought significant rain, mountain snow, strong thunderstorms and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 1.50 to 4.00 inches across coastal and valley areas and up to 7 inches across the mountain slopes. At higher elevations, over 1 foot of snowfall was reported across the Ventura and Los Angeles county mountains. Strong thunderstorms generated gusty outflow winds and heavy rain which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
