EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of S. Mendocino Ave. and E. Rose Ave.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sonoma County 911 dispatch was called on November 18 around 1:45pm PST after a caller reported seeing three people aboard a boat being battered by waves and waving a red flag for help. Four boaters were eventually rescued and taken to the hospital, an additional boater washed up dead on the shore. At the Bodega Bay buoy (46013), mean wave direction was NW with heights ranging between 13.5 and 15.7 feet and NW winds ranged between 11 and 18 knots during the 1:45pm timeframe on Monday (11/18).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sonoma County 911 dispatch was called on November 18 around 1:45pm PST after a caller reported seeing three people aboard a boat being battered by waves and waving a red flag for help. Four boaters were eventually rescued and taken to the hospital, an additional boater washed up dead on the shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Woodchuck Meadow SNOTEL (9209 feet) picked up an estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong swell waves in the eastern Pacific resulted in two fatal boating accidents in the waters off the North Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coast Guard received a report about six people on an overdue boat near Bodega Bay at 11:40 PM Saturday from the Sonoma County Sheriff's Office. The boaters departed Westside Marina on a 21-foot long blue and white vessel at 3 PM and were expected back by 7 PM. A 13-year old boy was found alive off South Salmon Creek Beach at 8 AM on Sunday, with an older boy found dead around 10:15 AM. The search was suspended around 6:30 PM pending new information.||Wind gusts at the Bodega Bay buoy (46013) reached around 27 knots during the afternoon and evening of Saturday the 2nd. Significant wave heights were 8 to 9.5 feet from the west-northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMPH Channel 26 mentioned a California Highway Patrol report of a funnel cloud just east of the intersection of SR 99 and Ave. 184.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks and mud in the roadway of SR 178 at the mouth of Kern River Canyon.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crane Flat RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on January 6 then dropped southeast into the Great Basin by the morning of January 7, A cold front that accompanied this system crossed central California during the evening of January 6 bringing a period of light to moderate precipitation and strong winds to the area. Several stations in the mountains and deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations exceeding 70 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beach Meadows SNOTEL (7650 feet) picked up an estimated 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river sank south and stalled over Humboldt, Del Norte, and western Trinity counties, bringing widespread rains between 3 and 7 inches to the region. Rain fell over a short period of time, 8-10 hours, resulting in the highest flows on the Mad River and Redwood Creek since the 1970s. Widespread creek flooding occurred in Del Norte and Humboldt counties with multiple homes damages or destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mad River stream gauge at Arcata exceeded flood stage and reached the 2nd highest stage ever recorded. Extensive flooding occurred of the Mad River Bottoms on both sides of the river. North Bank Road, Mad River Road, Silva Road, and Fischer Road all were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river sank south and stalled over Humboldt, Del Norte, and western Trinity counties, bringing widespread rains between 3 and 7 inches to the region. Rain fell over a short period of time, 8-10 hours, resulting in the highest flows on the Mad River and Redwood Creek since the 1970s. Widespread creek flooding occurred in Del Norte and Humboldt counties with multiple homes damages or destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smith River exceeded flood stage at the Dr. Fine Bridge gauge. Route 197 was flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest and snowiest storm of the season arrived at the end of February and continued into early March, bringing substantial disruptions to daily life. Widespread power outages and extended road closures were observed in the mountains and foothills from extremely heavy snow and strong, gusty southerly winds from February 29th through early March. Moderate to locally heavy rain was observed in the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported 4 to 16 inches over 24-hours starting February 29th through the morning of March 1st. Ski resorts in the Sierra reported snow amounts of 10 to 20 inches over the same timeframe. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 19.1 inches. On February 29th, wind gusts of 45 to 65 mph were observed in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest and snowiest storm of the season arrived at the end of February and continued into early March, bringing substantial disruptions to daily life. Widespread power outages and extended road closures were observed in the mountains and foothills from extremely heavy snow and strong, gusty southerly winds from February 29th through early March. Moderate to locally heavy rain was observed in the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS in Chester reported 2 inches of snow in 24-hours from February 29th through the morning of March 1st. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 24 hour snow amounts of 4 to 18 inches over the same timeframe. Weather stations reported wind gusts of 45 to 65 mph in the mountains on February 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor at Lower Lassen Peak reported 14.9 inches in 24-hours ending the morning of February 5th. Two CoCoRaHS in Chester reported total snow amounts of 6.4 and 7 inches. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated storm total snow amounts of 10 to 48 inches over the Western Plumas County and Lassen National Park Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 7 inches of new snowfall near Bear Valley Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of SR 58 and Cottonwood Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm, occluding frontal system brought snowfall to elevations above 4500 feet in northern California on 02/13 into 02/14.  This front hung up over the area then snowfall was enhanced by another strong system from 02/14 into 02/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 4 to 14 inches at elevations above 4500 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone ranged from 0.27 inches at Tennant South to 0.41 inches at Round Mountain, with higher amounts in the higher terrain near to Mount Shasta.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Panoche Road RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out behind the frontal boundary which slowly moved through the area between the afternoon of February 20 and the morning of February 21. With low levels remaining moist from recent rainfall and clearing skies and light winds prevailing, some areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley and in the Indian Wells Valley toward daybreak causing travel impacts on several highways. The fog mixed out by 900 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Visalia and Porterville. Several web cameras indicated visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered over the Desert Southwest provided for a warm and dry airmass over the area. With plenty of residual low level moisture, light wind and clearing skies, an area of dense fog formed during the morning of February 24 along the SR 99 corridor between Kingsburg and Tulare. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 as well as along SR 198 through Visalia where webcams indicated visibility was below 1000 feet. The fog mixed out by 830 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several web cameras indicated visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Winds and Widespread Precipitation Impact Southern California on March 2. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains and along the desert slopes, where gusts over 70 mph occurred. Widely beneficial precipitation fell, but reports of flooding were received in the San Bernardino County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Riverside County on March 2. The mountains saw accumulations of 0.50-1.80, while lower elevations in the Inland Empire saw 0.20-0.80. The lower deserts saw minimal accumulations.

Two juveniles were caught in the debris, fell down the hillside and died. Heavy rainfall impacted the area from February 18-21, 2024, with observed rainfall totals of 3-8 inches near the site of the incident.

Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest and snowiest storm of the season arrived at the end of February and continued into early March, bringing substantial disruptions to daily life. Widespread power outages and extended road closures were observed in the mountains and foothills from extremely heavy snow and strong, gusty southerly winds from February 29th through early March. Moderate to locally heavy rain was observed in the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS at a similar elevation measured snow amounts around 1 inch in 24 hours. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated snow amounts of 1 to 6 inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stockton Airport reported a max gust of 60 mph. Modesto Airport reported a max gust of 55 mph. California Highway Patrol reported multiple power lines down, downed trees, and road closures. Stanislaus County OES reported short-term power outages from the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported numerous downed trees and power lines due to high wind. A station in Placerville recorded a max gust of 64 mph. Nevada County Air Park reported a max gust of 52 mph. Calaveras Airport reported a max gust of 51 mph. Calaveras County OES reported scattered power outages throughout the county, removal of 55 downed trees from county roads, and 1 tree on a home. Tuolumne County OES reported multiple downed trees along roadways. Nevada County OES reported widespread power outages and downed trees across roads and into structures,  with moderate impacts to telecommunications. El Dorado County OES reported 40 calls for service related to trees and wires down, at least 5 buildings struck by trees, and 1 uninjured person trapped in a mobile home from a tree on top of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of an estimated 7 inches of new snowfall in Greeley Hill (3300 ft).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite 49 measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dana Meadows SNOTEL (9800 feet) picked up an estimated 14 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Bear Valley Springs measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With high pressure prevailing over California during the morning of February 12 and clear skies and light winds prevailing over the San Joaquin Valley, some patches of dense fog formed in the valley. Travel was briefly impacted on SR 99 between Merced and Atwater. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm, occluding frontal system brought snowfall to elevations above 4500 feet in northern California on 02/13 into 02/14.  This front hung up over the area then snowfall was enhanced by another strong system from 02/14 into 02/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 5 to 15 inches at elevations above 4500 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone ranged from 0.65 inches at Mt. Shasta City to 1.54 inches at Snowman and 1.71 inches at McCloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported large rocks and tree debris on eastbound SR 178 1 to 2 miles west of mile marker 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that moved southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska and brought lowering snow levels and gusty winds with heavy snow to northern California continued east of the Cascades early on 03/01, and was followed by a strong trough associated with the axis of an upper low. This slow moving secondary low brought additional heavy snow, lingering longest in the western portion of Siskiyou County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 9 to 36 inches at elevations above 3000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.70 inches at Quartz Hill, 1.74 inches at Dutch Indy, and 1.13 inches at Fort Jones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Charlotte Lake SNOTEL (11961 feet) picked up an estimated 24 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. The low continued to drop further southward on March 30 which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snowfall above 5000 feet in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 10 and 20 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while the Sierra foothills picked up between half an inch to an inch and half of rainfall. The higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between one and two inches of liquid precipitation. The low finally moved onshore over northern Baja on March 31 resulting in the precipitation slowly tapering off. Some thunderstorms did impact the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of March 31 producing gusty winds  and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of an estimated 6 inches of new snowfall 11NNE Springville at 3458 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported trees and power lines downed along south Union Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-period swell from the Central Pacific arrived at the Southern California beaches on February 20th. The swell was strong enough to produce breaking wave heights of 10-12 feet at many of the local beaches. Some localized instances of beach and coastal erosion also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntington Beach Lifeguards reported maximum surf heights of up to 12 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system approached the southern California coast during the afternoon of March 6 and moved across southern California during the evening. The low pulled up some moisture from a subtropical jet stream northward into central California and with the proximity of the upper low to the area destabilizing the airmass, showers and thunderstorms broke out over the area during the afternoon on March 6 and continued through the morning of March 7. The showers were most noticeable over the south end of the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. The snow level ranged between 5500 and 6000 feet during this event and a few inches of new snowfall fell above the snow line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on SR 198 near Goldenrod Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward over Nevada and into southeast California on March 14. The winds decreased in intensity by the evening of March 14 and dissipated by the afternoon of March 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Tom RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jawbone Station measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on SR 43 north of Kent Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest and snowiest storm of the season that started on February 29th lingered into early March, bringing blizzard conditions to the Sierra with heavy mountain snow and strong, gusty winds. Rain and thunderstorms were also observed. Extended road closures and chain restrictions were observed in the mountains, with widespread power outages. Pacific Gas and Electric reported approximately 280,000 customers impacted due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported storm total amounts of 106 inches at Kingvale, 116 inches at Soda Springs, 31 inches at Blue Canyon, and 66 inches at Eagle Lakes from February 29th through the morning of March 4th. Ski resorts measured snow amounts of 60 to 120 inches over the same timeframe. The UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported a storm total amount of 75.2 inches. Weather stations generally measured wind gusts of 50 to 80 mph across the mountains, with higher gusts at the higher peaks. There was a report of 114 mph gust at Duncan RAWS, and 171 mph gust at 2.0 SW Alpine Meadows.

Pacific Gas and Electric reported approximately 280,000 customers impacted due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Chester reported storm total snow amounts of 9 inches from February 29th through the morning of March 4th. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated snow amounts of 1 to 8 feet over the same timeframe. Weather stations reported wind gusts of 50 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough moved through central California on April 25 and 26. This system produced scattered showers across the area during the morning of April 26 and also brought a period of strong winds across the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County between the afternoon of April 25 and the evening of April 26 where several stations reported gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations across the remainder of the Kern County mountains and deserts reported gusts exceeding 45 mph during this same time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. The low continued to drop further southward on March 30 which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snowfall above 5000 feet in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 10 and 20 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while the Sierra foothills picked up between half an inch to an inch and half of rainfall. The higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between one and two inches of liquid precipitation. The low finally moved onshore over northern Baja on March 31 resulting in the precipitation slowly tapering off. Some thunderstorms did impact the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of March 31 producing gusty winds  and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bishop Pass SNOTEL (11972 feet) picked up an estimated 20 inches of new snowfall.

This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paradise Meadow SNOTEL (7676 feet) picked up an estimated 37 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blackcap Basin SNOTEL (10300 feet) picked up an estimated 23 inches of new snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR 138 wash out. State Highway is closed due to damage from flooding. 3 gauges received 4 inches of rain from 1 am to 5 pm March 2. Caltrans reported the road was undermined from water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest and snowiest storm of the season that started on February 29th lingered into early March, bringing blizzard conditions to the Sierra with heavy mountain snow and strong, gusty winds. Rain and thunderstorms were also observed. Extended road closures and chain restrictions were observed in the mountains, with widespread power outages. Pacific Gas and Electric reported approximately 280,000 customers impacted due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS in Grass Valley and Nevada City measured snow amounts of 4 to 6 inches. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimates snow amounts of 4 inches to a foot of snow. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a telephone pole down on Rd. 140 near Ave. 352.

Heavy snow and a high avalanche risk hindered search efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Post Pile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 25 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. The low continued to drop further southward on March 30 which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snowfall above 5000 feet in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 10 and 20 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while the Sierra foothills picked up between half an inch to an inch and half of rainfall. The higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between one and two inches of liquid precipitation. The low finally moved onshore over northern Baja on March 31 resulting in the precipitation slowly tapering off. Some thunderstorms did impact the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of March 31 producing gusty winds  and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported poles down on Randall Rd. near SR 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moving southward along the Lower Colorado River Valley results in strong northerly winds across most of southeast CA during the morning and afternoon hours on the 14th, with the nearby observing sites reporting peak gusts in excess of 30-40 mph. In addition, the exterior door to the utility room that faced the north-facing wall of the home was badly damaged. No injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low approached the northern California coast on March 22. The associated trough with this system pushed into central California by the evening bringing widespread precipitation to the area. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between 0.75 inches and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation while several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 10 to 15 inches of new snowfall. Most of the precipitation occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 23. Moderate rainfall in the Fresno area resulted in a fatal accident on SR 99 near the intersection of Clovis Ave. This system also brought a period of gusty winds to the Mojave Desert slopes between the evening of March 23 and the morning of March 24 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenaya Lake SNOTEL (8150 feet) picked up an estimated 17 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that Interstate 5 was closed for one hour near Lebec due to snow and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low approached the northern California coast on March 22. The associated trough with this system pushed into central California by the evening bringing widespread precipitation to the area. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between 0.75 inches and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation while several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 10 to 15 inches of new snowfall. Most of the precipitation occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 23. Moderate rainfall in the Fresno area resulted in a fatal accident on SR 99 near the intersection of Clovis Ave. This system also brought a period of gusty winds to the Mojave Desert slopes between the evening of March 23 and the morning of March 24 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Postpile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Rosamond 20W measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

While lesser rain fell across San Diego County, locally heavy rain rates lead to several traffic incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beech Meadows SNOTEL (7650 feet) picked up an estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through central California on March 1 and 2, with gusty winds and scattered thunderstorms. Radar indicated rotation in Monterey Bay would result in the first Tornado Warnings issued by the Monterey/San Francisco Bay Area office since March 2011. No damage was noted in a subsequent survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in the number 1 lane of southbound US Highway 101 north of the Correctional Training Facility between Gonzales and Soledad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low with an associated mature cyclone came to California on March 29, with widespread rain associated with the system. Continuing instability the next day resulted in noticeable thunderstorms with hail reported in several places across the Bay Area, including Walnut Creek, Belmont, and south of San Jose. In Monterey County, significant rainfall contributed to a landslide that forced the closure of California Highway 1 north of Big Sur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide along California Highway 1 near the Rocky Creek Bridge on March 30 forced the closure of the road at Palo Colorado. The landslide took out parts of the southbound lane of Highway 1 with the northbound lane also in danger. Combined with earlier closures of Highway 1 at Limekiln State Park near Lucia and the Nacimiento-Ferguson Road due to the Dolan Fire, the closure left the area around Big Sur isolated from the outside world. As of March 31, vehicles are only allowed to travel to the north in corridors at 8 AM and 4 PM daily.||The event followed a very active 48 hours of weather with heavy rain, strong winds and a rare severe thunderstorm warning. MRMS rainfall rates were as high as 1.9/hour on the 29th and a nearby gauge recorded 3.9 in 48 hours. Winds were also strong, with radar velocity data suggesting up to 60 mph gusts and nearby observations as high as 48 mph. A gale warning was issued for the coastal zone at 235AM on Thursday, March 28th. Valid 9AM-9PM Friday, easily verified by radar data, but no in situ observations are available. A wind advisory was issued at 205AM Thursday, March 28th and upgraded to a high wind warning at 215AM on Friday, March 29th. The high wind warning was valid 1AM-9PM Friday, and verified by a 62 mph gusts recorded at Lime Creek Road (741 ft). 5 separate Special Marine Warnings were issued for the affected coastline on Friday for winds greater than 40 kts and possible waterspouts. The issue times were 1226PM, 143PM, 243PM, 430PM, and 457PM. Again, these were only verified with radar data due to limited in-situ measurements over the water. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued at 312PM Friday for 60 mph wind gusts and a possible tornado based on radar indicated rotation. There were no reports of a tornado, and the highest observed wind gusts at the time of the warning were 46 mph, although a 62 mph gust was recorded at 540PM, after the warning expired (see high wind warning above). A Flood Advisory was issued at 448PM Friday that covered the slide area for urban and small stream flooding. While most of the severe weather in this area occurred on Friday, there were 2 Special Weather Statements issued at 221AM and 402AM for 30-40 mph wind and pea sized hail just south of the slide. The first was verified with a 31mph gust at 250AM from Pfeiffer Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tamarack Summit SNOTEL (7613 feet) picked up an estimated 25 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low with an associated mature cyclone came to California on March 29, with widespread rain associated with the system. Continuing instability the next day resulted in noticeable thunderstorms with hail reported in several places across the Bay Area, including Walnut Creek, Belmont, and south of San Jose. In Monterey County, significant rainfall contributed to a landslide that forced the closure of California Highway 1 north of Big Sur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximate location. Pea sized hail was reported in the Lexington Hills/Redwood Estates area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Farewell Gap SNOTEL (9500 feet) picked up an estimated 16 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. The low continued to drop further southward on March 30 which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snowfall above 5000 feet in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 10 and 20 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while the Sierra foothills picked up between half an inch to an inch and half of rainfall. The higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between one and two inches of liquid precipitation. The low finally moved onshore over northern Baja on March 31 resulting in the precipitation slowly tapering off. Some thunderstorms did impact the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of March 31 producing gusty winds  and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Bull SNOTEL (8073 feet) picked up an estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river took aim at Southern California for several days starting early on February 4th, with impacts lasting through February 9th. Over a foot of rain fell in some locations, while some areas in the mountains received several feet of accumulating snow, depending on elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Back-to-back atmospheric river events impacted Orange County starting early on February 4th, with weather impacts occurring through much of the week, and finally ending on February 9th. Much of Orange County received anywhere from 2-9 inches of rainfall. Some localized higher totals include 11.81 at Santiago Peak, numerous other locations in the Santa Ana Mountains including Holy Jim Canyon which received 8-10 inches of rain, and 8.94 of rain at the Coto De Caza (CTDC1) observation site. Coastal areas generally received 2-5 inches of rain, while inland areas generally received rainfall totals in the 5-8 range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree on SR 65 1NW Porterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought precipitation and thunderstorm impacts to Southern California March 14th through the 16th. The main weather impacts were due to flooding and mountain snow, but a significant amount of lightning was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalTrans report of flooding in the number 4 lane on I-15 South north of Highway 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving across the Desert Southwest combined with well-above normal moisture, with precipitable water values peak at 1.0-1.2, resulted in a large swath of rain with embedded thunderstorms moving across portions of southeast CA during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 22nd. Rainfall rates ranged between 0.10-0.50 according to doppler radar estimates, resulting in total amounts of between 0.5-1.25 within a period of three hours. The rainfall was enough to result in areas of flooding , causing several road closures. High water levels along the New River in Calexico caused damage to an Improvement Project that was being performed along the river itself. A state of emergency was declared for both the city of El Centro and Calexico according to the Imperial County Emergency Manager.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ogilby Road closed between Interstate 8 and State Route 78 due to overflowing washes causing flooding on the road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New River USGS Gauge at the International Border in Calexico reported water levels peaking to 15.6 feet, which is just above minor flood stage. The rise to minor flood stage was generally gradual as water levels rose at a rate of 1 feet per hour according to the gauge data. The rising water levels caused damage to an improvement project being performed at the New River in Calexico with water overtopping the sheet piles. About 10 feet of standing water was estimated inside the sheet piles. This water overtopping caused a generator controlling the dewatering to flood and shutdown. The city of Calexico declared an emergency due to the damages that the high water levels caused to the project. According to an interview performed with a city councilmember  approximately two weeks after the incident, the damage cost estimate was at around 4 million dollars. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate stream north of mid-level monsoonal moisture coupled with instability and daytime heating enabled a 40-60% chance for thunderstorms during this brief period. Storms initiated over Mono County afternoon of the 2nd and worked their way north into northeast CA into the evening. There was a 40% chance of nocturnal thunderstorms across far western NV and northeast CA. Storms were projected to have the potential to produce localized flash flooding under stronger, training storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station XMLC1 (Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, reported  a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: PG&E weather stations reported gusts between 50 and 70 mph throughout the northern reaches of Clear Lake including in the towns of Nice and Lucerne, the Robinson Rancheria,  and south toward Mt. Konocti. Numerous trees were downed and power was out to thousands of people across the region. Additional strong winds combined with snow led to long duration power outages around Cobb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Post Pile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Post Pile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 22 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS OF SAN BENITO COUNTY AND INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY INCLUDING PINNACLES NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone and atmospheric river combined from February 3rd to the 5th, creating a period of strong winds, heavy rain, and high surf. The entire region saw numerous impacts including roadway flooding, fallen trees, and power outages. Flood prone areas near rivers and along the coast were evacuated. Notable impacts also included pre-emptive closing of state parks, the cancelation of the San Francisco Half-Marathon, and the PGA canceling the final round of the Pebble Beach Pro-Am golf tournament. On Monday February 5, several schools were closed due to damage from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lone Tree Towers station operated by PG&E recorded a wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone and atmospheric river combined from February 3rd to the 5th, creating a period of strong winds, heavy rain, and high surf. The entire region saw numerous impacts including roadway flooding, fallen trees, and power outages. Flood prone areas near rivers and along the coast were evacuated. Notable impacts also included pre-emptive closing of state parks, the cancelation of the San Francisco Half-Marathon, and the PGA canceling the final round of the Pebble Beach Pro-Am golf tournament. On Monday February 5, several schools were closed due to damage from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide blocked Market Street at 18th Street in San Francisco, causing the closure of westbound Market Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cyclone bombed very close to the central California coast developing widespread damaging wind gusts across Mendocino and Lake counties. Heavy rain also was observed with some of the highest flows on creeks in Lake County in the last several years. The low then moved north off of the extreme northwestern California coast bringing strong gusts into Humboldt and Del Norte counties as well, though damage was limited.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PG&E weather station on Cahto Peak reported a peak wind gust of 76 mph. Numerous other stations reported wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph with widespread power outages occurring across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that the off ramp and ramp is under water on Interstate 80 West and Penryn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems impacted central California From February 17 to 20. A quickly-moving cold front came through the region on the 17th, causing minor nuisance flooding and gusty winds. A second, deeper but slower moving, system followed the next day with widespread rain and gusty winds. A warm front associated with this low came through the region late on Sunday the 18th, followed by the associated cold front and associated thunderstorms on Monday the 19th. With the longer period of rainfall and soils being saturated, shallow landslides were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding was reported at California Route 1 and Kruse Ranch Road. A vehicle was stuck in mud on the right hand side of the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski resorts reported snow amounts of 15 to 30 inches from February 1st through February 2nd. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 14.6 inches of snow over the same timeframe. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 10 to 30 inches of snow as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding of Rawson Road and Flores Avenue.
