EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Lakes SNOTEL (10419 feet) picked up an estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chagoopa Plateau SNOTEL (9450 feet) picked up an estimated 42 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems impacted central California From February 17 to 20. A quickly-moving cold front came through the region on the 17th, causing minor nuisance flooding and gusty winds. A second, deeper but slower moving, system followed the next day with widespread rain and gusty winds. A warm front associated with this low came through the region late on Sunday the 18th, followed by the associated cold front and associated thunderstorms on Monday the 19th. With the longer period of rainfall and soils being saturated, shallow landslides were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized flooding was reported around Point Reyes Station and Inverness Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slider-type shortwave and attendant moisture were forecast to bring a modest |with Sierra snowfall staying on the lighter side compared to previous February winter storms. Snowfall totals along the Sierra crest were expected to vary from 3-6 with lower elevation Sierra communities (Truckee, South Lake Tahoe, Bridgeport, Mammoth Lakes) seeing only 1-3 inches through Thursday morning. There was a 10% chance for these locales seeing up to 6 inches of snow in a boom scenario. Bands of moderate snowfall with this winter system in fact did bring a modest ���Boom��� storm total snowfall to some areas south of US-50 to include the Eastern Sierra, Mono County, southern Lyon County, and Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA and the June Lake Ski Base, reported 24 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 9.0 inches and 7.0 inches respectively.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beach Meadows SNOTEL (7650 feet) picked up an estimated 16 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems impacted central California From February 17 to 20. A quickly-moving cold front came through the region on the 17th, causing minor nuisance flooding and gusty winds. A second, deeper but slower moving, system followed the next day with widespread rain and gusty winds. A warm front associated with this low came through the region late on Sunday the 18th, followed by the associated cold front and associated thunderstorms on Monday the 19th. With the longer period of rainfall and soils being saturated, shallow landslides were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The number 3 and 4 lanes of southbound Interstate 680 were flooded just south of McKee Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Lakes SNOTEL (10419 feet) picked up and estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone and atmospheric river combined from February 3rd to the 5th, creating a period of strong winds, heavy rain, and high surf. The entire region saw numerous impacts including roadway flooding, fallen trees, and power outages. Flood prone areas near rivers and along the coast were evacuated. Notable impacts also included pre-emptive closing of state parks, the cancelation of the San Francisco Half-Marathon, and the PGA canceling the final round of the Pebble Beach Pro-Am golf tournament. On Monday February 5, several schools were closed due to damage from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell into a home in Boulder Creek on the 14100 block of California Highway 9 near the intersection with Bear Creek Road. The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office reported that fire crews arrived around 3:20 PM. Two people were inside the home when the tree fell. One person escaped, but a 45-year old man was trapped inside. The man was killed after the tree fell and died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved eastward across the Pacific Northwest and Northern California on May 4 and 5. This system brought widespread precipitation to our area between the early afternoon of May 4 and the morning of May 5 as moisture pushed southward through central California with much of the Sierra Nevada from Sierra National Park northward picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 6 to 12 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County received between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall. While snow levels generally remained above 6000 feet for most of the event, elevations as low as 3000 feet received a dusting of snow during the morning of May 5. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski summit, CA, reported 48 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 14.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moving through the Pacific Northwest on May 28 pushed a shortwave system through central California during the evening of May 28 and morning of May 29 producing an increased onshore pressure gradient. This in turn resulted in a period of strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert slopes during the evening of May 28 and the early morning of May 29. Several stations reported gusts between 45 and 60 mph during this time frame. The winds decreased by late morning on May 29 as gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of winter storms that was directed a bit more south of Lake Tahoe along the eastern Sierra into Mono county. This system brought additional snow amounts to much of the Sierra late Sunday into Monday with periods of lower elevation/valley rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds. The main area of focus shifted from the Tahoe basin to Mono county where the White Mountains looked to get 2-3 inches of new snow while Mammoth Mountain was projected to receive another 5 to 10 inches of fresh snow. Snow levels hovered around 7000 feet for much of this second winter storm, then lowered to near 6000 feet towards the tail end of the storm late Monday. ECMWF EFI signals for CAPE across western Nevada showed higher instability with this second storm that indicated a better spillover potential and for showers to  persist well into Monday afternoon and evening. Storm liquid precipitation totals with this storm was projected to be about 1.50 inches to 2.00 inches along the span of the Sierra crest that translated to another 1 to 2 plus feet of snowfall for elevations above 8000 feet with this second storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at Palisades Tahoe summit, CA,  reported a peak wind gust of 97 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather returns Wednesday afternoon to close out the month of January. Therefore, strong and gusty winds especially over higher mountain elevations was followed with mountain snow and valley rain showers. Wind was the first hazard expected starting over the Sierra and NE CA, then spreading into western Nevada with south winds between 30-50 mph, and gusts over 60-80 mph across higher mountain ridges and passes. Valley winds were projected to be 15-35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The probability of 60 mph winds across mountain areas in CA was 60% or greater and 10-20% over W NV. Lake winds were around 15-25 mph with gusts to 35-45 mph.||Valley rain and mountain snow showers were projected to move into the region   Wednesday PM. Global models and their respective ensemble members initially dug the trough and a deeper layer of sub-tropical IVT moisture will quickly sweep east and south across portions of northeast California, before descending into the Sierra, western Nevada, and Mono Co. Snow levels started near 7000 feet on Wednesday, then dropped to 6000 feet by Thursday. Most higher elevation areas received mostly rain initially before transitioning to mostly snow Thursday evening. Snow levels dropped to 5500 ft on Thursday afternoon, and dropped further to below 4000 feet by Friday morning. The Sierra above 7000 and Mono Co were given even chances for about 1-2 feet of new snow with 6-12 inches below 7000 feet. For northeast California 4-10 inches of accumulated snow, and up to 20 inches in the higher mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (LDRC1-Ladder Butte) located 17.0 mi S of Pittville, CA, and mesonet station Snag Hill Lookout (216PG) located 12.0 mi SSE of Bieber, CA, reported peak wind gusts of 65 mph and 59 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 89 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 30 year old female was swept away by cold and fast moving currents along the San Joaquin River near Newman on May 9th. She drowned, and her body was found on May 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On May 9th, a 30 year old female was watching her children play in the water and noticed her 11 year old daughter struggling to keep her head above water. The 30 year old female then jumped into the river to help, but also struggled to stay afloat. Soon after seeing this, her 14 year old son then jumped in the water to help. The 14 year old and 11 year old were able to make it safely back to shore, but the 30 year old female did not. At 6:30 PM on May 9th, a 911 call was received for a water rescue in the San Joaquin River. Then, at 5:30 PM on May 11th, the body of the 30 year old female was recovered in the San Joaquin River by a volunteer diver. Water temperatures in the mid 60s to low 70s between Crows Landing and Hills Ferry over the weekend. San Joaquin River - Newman gage indicated river stage around 51.8 feet on May 9th at 5 PM, with stage gradually rising to 53.8 feet by Saturday, May 11th at 6:45 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 55-year old male was found dead off Point Reyes on May 1. The deceased was last seen with a surfboard around 10AM at Drakes Beach off of Point Reyes National Seashore. His surfboard was found washed ashore at Drakes Beach at approximately 1:00PM with 9-1-1 first called at approximately 2:36PM. First responders shortly began the search and a Search and Rescue Team was dispatched. A search and rescue spot forecast was requested at 5:15PM and completed at approximately 5:28PM. Search and Rescue teams located the body at 5:33PM in the ocean approximately 2.5 miles northwest of the Drakes Beach Parking Area. The surfer was pronounced deceased at 5:35PM and identified. Local buoy data from Buoy 46013 reported sustained winds between 20-25 knots and gusts between 30-40 knots between 9AM to 5PM on 1 May. Wave heights were reported to be between 9 to 10 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather returned on the 4th with a storm system that brought gusty winds along with valley rain and mountain snow. The storm trajectory allowed a quick moving cold front to sweep across the region early morning on the 4th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media report of a funnel cloud over Allen Rd. between Hageman Rd. and Rosedale Hwy. in Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought precipitation and thunderstorm impacts to Southern California March 14th through the 16th. The main weather impacts were due to flooding and mountain snow, but a significant amount of lightning was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought heavy snow to portions of the San Bernardino County Mountains March 14-15. Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred in the Pine Cove area, reports ranging from 6100' to 6500' in elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large tree branch in the southbound lane of S. Clovis Ave. near the intersection of E. Butler Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding and mud debris from construction east of this location were observed.

Most areas saw 1-3 inches, with locations near Snow Summit, Snow Valley, Green Valley Lake and Forest Falls seeing up to 4 inches in accumulations. Numerous vehicle incidents were reported in the mountains as a result of snow covered roads.

Due to temperatures falling the upper 20s and the first snowfall, numerous spin outs occurred in Big Bear Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the Riverside County Mountains on January 3, with accumulations as low as 5400 feet reported. Most areas saw 1-3 inches, with Long Valley Mount San Jacinto seeing up to 6 inches of snow. Thundersnow was reported near Mount San Jacinto 4-6pm on January 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought precipitation and thunderstorm impacts to Southern California March 14th through the 16th. The main weather impacts were due to flooding and mountain snow, but a significant amount of lightning was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought heavy snow to portions of the San Bernardino County Mountains March 14-15. Some notable storm total snowfall amounts include: 14 inches at Baldwin Lake (6700' in elevation), 13 inches at Bear Mountain Snow Summit (7500'), and 12 inches at Snow Valley (7000').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river took aim at Southern California for several days starting early on February 4th, with impacts lasting through February 9th. Over a foot of rain fell in some locations, while some areas in the mountains received several feet of accumulating snow, depending on elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 year old man drowned in the Tijuana River Channel on February 6 while attempting to cross the international border, following heavy rain in San Diego. San Diego Fire-Rescue swift water crews pulled the body from the river and despite attempted lifesaving measures, the man was pronounced dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active month continued on March 23-24, as another storm system brought more precipitation impacts to Southern California. The main impacts were in the form of snow at some mountain communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm brought another round of thunderstorms and snow to the mountain communities in San Bernardino County. Some of the more notable snow totals include: 8 inches at Snow Valley (7000-8000') and 5 inches at Green Valley Lake (6900').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of 6 inches of water flowing over a roadway 30 feet wide. Water was up to the bumpers of cars.

Rainfall rates in at the time of report were 1.30 inches per hour.||Northbound I-15 at Main Street was fully closed around 1:30 PM due to flooding, and drivers were at a standstill in the flooded roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 13 July, forcing provided by an upper low off the central CA coast, and a southerly flow aloft riding around the outer western periphery of an upper ridge centered over Four-Corners region pulled deep monsoonal moisture northward into the Sierra and western Nevada. These conditions initiated scattered showers and thunderstorms across much of the region. As these weather features showed little movement, a near repeat scenario occurred for 14 July. Showers with embedded storms and lightning lingered through the night into the morning hours. The return of a southwest flow that evening across the Sierra kicked the storms into west central NV with the activity winding down later that night. Residual moisture was slower to scour out the afternoon of 15 July south of US-50 that resulted in a few short lived showers and initiation of pulse-type thunderstorms. The typical array of storm effects included locally heavy rainfall, lightning, outflow gusts of 35+ knots, small hail, and lower CIGS/VIS with mountain obscurations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet stations ICKC1(SNOTEL) located 3.6 mi N of Independence Lake, CA,  RNYC (Rice Canyon) located 5.0 mi SSE of Sierraville, CA, and ICPC1(SNOTEL) located 0.9 mi NE of Independence Lake, CA reported a total rainfall amounts of 1.10 inches, 0.72 inches, and 0.6 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of mountain snow with a valley rain and snow mix will continued through the holiday weekend with this second of two impactful late season storms. This system brought travel regional impacts especially over Sierra mountain passes. The low pressure system that rotated cyclonically just off-shore the central CA coast enabled a south-southwest upper flow to tap into a thick layer of Pacific moisture that spread north and northeast into the Sierra and western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 1.0 mi SSW of Northstar Ski Base , CA, CoCoRAHS Station CA-ED-9 located 0.5 mi S of Tahoma, CA, and the Central Sierra Snow Lab located 6.0 mi W of Truckee, CA, reported 24 hour snowfall amounts of  14.0 inches, 12.3 inches, and 9.8 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third, powerful, winter storm brought significant impacts to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged between 2 and 12 inches with the highest totals across southern Santa Barbara county and western Ventura county. With the heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flooding and mud/debris flows were reported. Also, some gusty winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall generated a mudslide across River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of otherwise quieter and dry conditions, a weaker appetizer storm pushed through the region February 16, that brought more wind than either rain or snow to the region. Periods of travel difficulties, however, were still expected for Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This comparatively weaker winter storm opened up another active weather pattern that tracked through the Sierra and W.Nevada late in the evening February 17-18. This storm brought higher elevation snowfall that was heavy at times above 7000 feet. This storm also included the possibility of an additional several feet of snowfall.  Mainly spillover rainfall fell over lower western Nevada valleys that included periods of gusty winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA, Main Lodge, reported a 72 hour storm total snowfall amount at the Ski Base of 28 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather returned on the 4th with a storm system that brought gusty winds along with valley rain and mountain snow. The storm trajectory allowed a quick moving cold front to sweep across the region early morning on the 4th. This front set the region up to see two impactful weather concerns: gusty to strong southwesterly winds along with winter-like precipitation that included accumulated mountain snow and valley rain. Conditions slowly improved on  the 5th with a slow warming trend and low precipitation chances following early the next week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, and at Tahoe City, CA, as well as Boca Reservoir, CA,  reported 24 hour total snowfall amounts of  26.0 inches, 12.0 inches, and 6.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, very strong, winter storm impacted Southwestern California. Rainfall totals were very significant across the area. Los Angeles county reported rain totals of 4 to 14 inches, Ventura county reported 2 to 10 inches, Santa Barbara county reported 4 to 12 inches and San Luis Obispo county reported 2 to 5 inches. With the heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flooding were received. In the mountains, very significant snowfall was reported with amounts in the 1 to 3 foot range. Also, severe thunderstorms and tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant debris flow, over 1 foot of mud, was reported on Coldwater Canyon Road between Mulholland Drive and Sunset Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This first of two impactful weather systems arrived in the area during afternoon on the 27th with the passage of a cold front. Gusty to strong winds across the region as well as valley rain and mountain snow accompanied this initial storm system through the morning of the 28th. Following a brief, a second impactful system brought additional precipitation chances for mountain snow and valley rain that started overnight on the 29th that continued through the Easter weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, and Mesonet station (HVRTM - Blue Streak Weather Station) located 1.0 mi NNW of Heavenly Ski Summit reported peak wind gusts of 127 mph and 63 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough that dug across the interior West Saturday and Sunday, |aligned with the left exit region of a meridional jet favoring large scale ascent across the Sierra and western Nevada. This setup for strong winds and widespread precipitation across the region. This included the potential for heavy Sierra snowfall with light snow accumulation on valley floors. As the first snow arrived Saturday morning wind observations over Sierra ridges showed gusts ranging 70-80 mph. This system was considered the coldest and most impactful storm up to this point this season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Northstar Ski Base , CA, and on the upper mountain of the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, CA, reported 24 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 13.0 inches and 17.0 inches respectively.   Heavenly Ski Base, CA, reported a 24 hour storm total snowfall amount of 12.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire started around the afternoon of July 24, just east of Chico off Upper Park Road in Upper Bidwell Park. Very hot temperatures, combined with critical fire weather conditions allowed for rapid growth of the fire shortly after ignition prompting multiple evacuations warnings and orders. The fire continued to burn into August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire started around 2 PM on July 24, just east of Chico off Upper Park Road in Upper Bidwell Park. Very hot temperatures, combined with critical fire weather conditions allowed for rapid growth of the fire shortly after ignition prompting multiple evacuations warnings and orders. Within the first 24-30 hours, the fire was mapped at approximately 125,000 acres. |||Red Flag conditions were not being met at the time of ignition, however temperatures reached around 99 to 106 degrees, with relative humidity in the 14 to 25 percent range, and wind gusts of 10 to 25 mph. From July 24rd through the 31st, the heat continued with temperatures peaking around 94 to 106 degrees, and low relative humidity continued, in the 5 to 25 percent range, however winds returned to more of the typical diurnal regime. By July 31st, approximately 391,200 acres had burned with 434 structures destroyed and 39 structures damaged and 18% containment. Tehama County had 338,201 acres burned with 127 structures destroyed  and 4 structures damaged. Butte County burned 52,999 acres with 310 destroyed and 38 damaged. The fire was not contained in the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved eastward across the Pacific Northwest and Northern California on May 4 and 5. This system brought widespread precipitation to our area between the early afternoon of May 4 and the morning of May 5 as moisture pushed southward through central California with much of the Sierra Nevada from Sierra National Park northward picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 6 to 12 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County received between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall. While snow levels generally remained above 6000 feet for most of the event, elevations as low as 3000 feet received a dusting of snow during the morning of May 5. In addition to the precipitation, this system also brought unseasonably cool temperatures to the area on May 5 and 6 and also produced a period of strong wind gusts across the mountains and deserts in Kern County between the afternoon of May 4 and the early morning of May 6 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Postpile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration heat event brought dangerously hot conditions with widespread Major to Extreme HeatRisk to interior Northern California over the first two weeks of July. Multiple temperature records were broken or tied over this timeframe. The Sacramento County Coroner reported two fatalities due to the extreme heat, one on July 6th and one on July 7th. Temperatures cooled slightly for a brief period in the Southern Sacramento Valley, Delta and Carquinez Straight, and Northern San Joaquin Valley on July 8th and 9th, but conditions were still hot, before ramping up again on July 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously hot daytime high temperatures of 100 to 115 degrees were observed from July 2nd through July 13th. Little relief from the heat was observed overnight, with low temperatures around the mid 70s to upper 80s over the same timeframe.

Little relief from the heat was observed overnight, with low temperatures around the 70s to mid 80s over the same timeframe. An all time record high was set at Redding Municipal Airport on July 6th, reaching 119 degrees, breaking the previous record of 118 degrees. Multiple daily record high temperatures were tied or broken at Redding Municipal Airport, and Red Bluff Airport over the duration of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Shelly Wildfire was discovered on July 3rd at 327 PM PST. The fire started 8 miles west of Etna, in the Klamath National Forest. The cause of the fire is undetermined. As of  530 PST on 08/01, it had burned 15,520 acres, was 92% contained, no structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $ 106.7M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge of high pressure pushes inland into California on July 2. This ridge provided our area with a hot and dry airmass which persisted for several days until the ridge moved east and temperatures cooled below dangerous levels on July 14. Several record high maximum temperatures and high minimum temperatures were set between July 6 and July 8 and again on July 11 and 12. In addition to the persistent heat between July 2 and July 13, low daytime humidity with poor overnight recovery and heavy fuel loading resulted in an increased fire danger for much of the area during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the 12 day heat wave event, afternoon highs were mainly between 112 and 118 degrees on July 6 through July 8 and on July 11 and 12. Afternoon highs were mainly between 106 and 112 degrees on July 2 through July 5 and on July 9, 10 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge of high pressure pushes inland into California on July 2. This ridge provided our area with a hot and dry airmass which persisted for several days until the ridge moved east and temperatures cooled below dangerous levels on July 14. Several record high maximum temperatures and high minimum temperatures were set between July 6 and July 8 and again on July 11 and 12. In addition to the persistent heat between July 2 and July 13, low daytime humidity with poor overnight recovery and heavy fuel loading resulted in an increased fire danger for much of the area during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the 12 day heat wave event, afternoon highs were mainly between 112 and 118 degrees on July 6 through July 8 and on July 11 and 12. Afternoon highs were mainly between 106 and 112 degrees on July 2 through July 5 and on July 9, 10 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration heat event brought dangerously hot conditions with widespread Major to Extreme HeatRisk to interior Northern California over the first two weeks of July. Multiple temperature records were broken or tied over this timeframe. The Sacramento County Coroner reported two fatalities due to the extreme heat, one on July 6th and one on July 7th. Temperatures cooled slightly for a brief period in the Southern Sacramento Valley, Delta and Carquinez Straight, and Northern San Joaquin Valley on July 8th and 9th, but conditions were still hot, before ramping up again on July 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously hot daytime high temperatures of 105 to 115 degrees were observed from July 2nd through July 13th. Little relief from the heat was observed overnight, with low temperatures around the 70s to low 80s over the same timeframe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast flow around a high pressure center over southern Nevada brought some upper level tropical moisture to central California. With instability increasing over the Sierra Nevada during the afternoon scattered thunderstorms broke out, and some of them produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Incident Meteorologist reported burn scar debris flooding on Chimney Basin Rd. south of Kennedy Meadows. Field operations estimated 0.25 inches of rainfall triggered this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 13 July, forcing provided by an upper low off the central CA coast, and a southerly flow aloft riding around the outer western periphery of an upper ridge centered over Four-Corners region pulled deep monsoonal moisture northward into the Sierra and western Nevada. These conditions initiated scattered showers and thunderstorms across much of the region. As these weather features showed little movement, a near repeat scenario occurred for 14 July. Showers with embedded storms and lightning lingered through the night into the morning hours. The return of a southwest flow that evening across the Sierra kicked the storms into west central NV with the activity winding down later that night. Residual moisture was slower to scour out the afternoon of 15 July south of US-50 that resulted in a few short lived showers and initiation of pulse-type thunderstorms. The typical array of storm effects included locally heavy rainfall, lightning, outflow gusts of 35+ knots, small hail, and lower CIGS/VIS with mountain obscurations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station XXXC1(SNOTEL) located 7.7 mi SW of Coleville, CA reported a total rainfall amount of 0.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Thompson Fire started around 11 am on July 2nd, 2024 near the spillway of Lake Oroville Dam. Very hot temperatures, combined with critical fire weather conditions allowed for rapid growth of the fire shortly after ignition prompting several evacuation warnings and orders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thompson Fire started around 11 am on July 2nd, 2024 near the spillway of Lake Oroville Dam. Very hot temperatures combined with critical fire weather conditions allowed for rapid growth of the fire shortly after ignition prompting several evacuation warnings and orders. Within the first 24 hours, the fire was mapped at over 3,000 acres. The California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection (Cal Fire) reported the fire to be 100% contained on July 8th, 2024 at 3,789 acres. Red Flag conditions were not being met at the time of ignition, however temperatures reached around 100 to 104 degrees, with relative humidity in the 10 to 18 percent range, and wind gusts of 20 to 25 mph. From July 3rd through 8th, the heat continued with temperatures peaking around 110 to 115 degrees, and low relative humidity continued, in the 8 to 18 percent range, however winds returned to more of the typical diurnal regime. Over 10,000 people were under evacuation orders. The California Department of Water Resources activated the EAP for Lake Oroville Dam under non-emergency level. The fire resulted in loss of transmission lines. 8 structures were damaged, and 26 structures were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of upper level tropical moisture pushed into central California from the southeast during the morning of July 13 and continued for much of the day. At first the thunderstorms were mainly dry as the lower and middle levels remained dry, and lightning from these thunderstorms ignited several new wildfires across the area. As the middle levels became more moist during the late afternoon and evening the thunderstorms became more in the way of rain producers. There were some locations in Yosemite National Park and the Upper San Joaquin River Basin which picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall, but lower elevations generally only picked up a trace to a few hundredths of and inch of rain. There were also several reports of hail with one cell in Yosemite National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video evidence of 1.00 inch diameter (quarter sized) hail posted on Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Topical moisture streaming northward into central California triggered another round of afternoon and evening thunderstorms mainly impacting the Sierra Nevada and the Kern County Desert areas. While rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less, a  few locations in the south end of the upper Sierra and the Kern River Valley which were impacted by heavier thunderstorms measured over a quarter of an inch of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured 0.52 inches of rainfall between 1900 and 2000 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated shower and thunderstorm chances returned 20 July, and remained mostly south of Highway 50 with Mono-Mineral Counties being the focal points for activity. High-resolution ensemble guidance also showed the best chances for showers and thunderstorms mostly south of highway 50. Simulations projected a 30-40% chance for isolated showers and a few storms that would initiate mostly across Mono, southern Lyon, and Mineral counties around 1-2PM. There was also a chance (10-20%) that one or two storms would push northeast into eastern Churchill County as well. Storms quickly tapered off in the evening after sundown. Afternoon west-southwest breezes of 20-30 mph were expected to be slightly enhanced  in the vicinity of developing storms. The usual storm related culprits of copious lightning, as well as gusty and erratic outflows accompanied storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported minor flooding/debris flow 1.0 mi S of Conway Summit, CA, with rocks and a large boulder in the northbound lane of US-395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure built westward into California on July 21 and 22 which resulted in the return of dangerous heat across the Kern County Deserts on July 21 and across the San Joaquin Valley West Side Hills, Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley and Kern River Canyon by July 22. The heat continued through July 26 when an upper trough approached the California coast and brought cooler temperatures to the area as it moved inland on July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 108 and 113 degrees each day between July 21 and July 26.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 62 was closed between El Rey Ave and Encelia Ave, with at least two feet of water and debris over the road. Numerous cars were stuck, and swift water rescues were needed. Nearly three feet of water and mud flowed through a motel, destroying the first floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to western Siskiyou County for 10 consecutive days from July 4th through July 13th. Low temperatures were generally in the mid 50s to upper 60s, and high temperatures in the upper 90s to 110 degrees. But, peak readings were recorded on July 6th of 114 degrees at Happy Camp and Sawyers Bar, and 115 degrees at Somes Bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record 10 day heat wave occurred early to mid July 2024. Interior southern California, mountains and deserts, experience the hottest 2 week period for July, and July 2024 was the hottest month of record at Palm Springs. The average monthly temperature in Palm Springs was 100F for July. Borrego Springs average high was also a record at 97.5F. July was also the hottest month on record for the state of California, exceeding the previous year by 1.9F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were extreme the first 10 days of July. On July 5, 2024, Palm Spring (PSP) recorded a high of 124F which is an all-time high temperature. From July 1 to July 12, the Palm Springs airport had high temperatures of 114 to to 124 including a high of 122F on July 8th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anza Borrego State Park recorded high temperatures of 110 to 121F between July 1 to 13, 2024. On July 8th, 2024, a high temperature of 121F was recorded. This severe heat is anomalous even for a desert region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to central Siskiyou County for 10 consecutive days from July 4th through July 13th. Low temperatures were generally in the lower 60s to lower 70s, and high temperatures in the upper 90s to 105 degrees. But, peak readings were recorded on July 6th of 113 degrees at Hornbrook and Oak Knoll. Record high temperatures were set at Siskiyou County-Montague Airport of 104 degrees on July 4th, 108 degrees on July 5th, 111 degrees on July 6th, 109 degrees on July 7th, 109 degrees on July 8th, 108 degrees on July 9th, 107 degrees on July 12th, and 109 degrees on July 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to south central Siskiyou County for 9 consecutive days from July 5th through July 13th. Shasta RAWS, and 110 degrees at Mott. Record high temperatures were set at Mt. Shasta City of 104 degrees on July 6th, 104 degrees on July 7th, 101 degrees on July 8th, 101 degrees on July 12th, and 104 degrees on July 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure brought excessive heat to north central and southeast Siskiyou County during the peak of a heat wave from July 6th through July 7th. Low temperatures were in the lower 50s to lower 70s, and high temperatures in the mid 90s to 104 degrees.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent ridge over the western states with an associated thermal trough extending from northern California into southern Oregon brought a long-duration heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure brought excessive heat to northeast Siskiyou and northwest Modoc counties for 4 consecutive days from July 6th through July 9th, then temperatures were just a little below excessive values on July 10th and 11th. Excessive heat returned on July 12th and 13th. Low temperatures were generally in the lower 50s to upper 60s, and high temperatures in the mid 90s to 101 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain and a flash flood potential along with strong outflow gusts through Wednesday. Abundant moisture and instability were present as noted by the 0.81 PWAT reading from the 23 July 1200Z KREV sounding this morning. Sufficient clearing also helped temperatures to climb well above average for late July. Short term high-resolution HREF guidance showed an uptick in PWATs Wednesday as deeper moisture expanded northward across western NV and far northeast CA |as a ridge axis slid east with upper level southerly flow increasing ahead of a trough advancing into the Pacific Northwest. A few severe storms with gusts to 60 mph and nickel sized hail were forecasted on 23 July. But flash flooding would be the bigger concern as storms moved either slower or anchored near steep terrain and burn scars. For 24 July, mid level S/SW winds increased which increased both storm motions and shear for storm organization. SPC introduced a marginal risk for severe wind gusts near the Oregon border with similar severe weather threats for stronger outflows and blowing dust reaching the Basin and Range. While storms were expected to move faster, training storm  cells would continue to bring a heightened threat for flash flooding for areas south of I-80 with focus on the eastern Sierra into southern Lyon and Mineral counties. For 25 July, drier and more stable air confined storms to the Basin and Range where activity was forecast to be isolated but still able to produce gusty outflows and areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a debris flow located 1.0 mi SSE of Crestview, CA, with large boulders in the roadway due  to heavy rain in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record 10 day heat wave occurred early to mid July 2024. Interior southern California, mountains and deserts, experience the hottest 2 week period for July, and July 2024 was the hottest month of record at Palm Springs. The average monthly temperature in Palm Springs was 100F for July. Borrego Springs average high was also a record at 97.5F. July was also the hottest month on record for the state of California, exceeding the previous year by 1.9F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the mountains were in the 90s to around 100F between July 1 and July 12, 2024.  Highs reached as hot as 103 to 109F up to elevation 4000 feet, especially on July 5, 10 and 11, 2024. Big Bear Lake at 7000 feet had high temperatures in the lower 90s.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge of high pressure pushes inland into California on July 2. This ridge provided our area with a hot and dry airmass which persisted for several days until the ridge moved east and temperatures cooled below dangerous levels on July 14. Several record high maximum temperatures and high minimum temperatures were set between July 6 and July 8 and again on July 11 and 12. In addition to the persistent heat between July 2 and July 13, low daytime humidity with poor overnight recovery and heavy fuel loading resulted in an increased fire danger for much of the area during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the 12 day heat wave event, afternoon highs were mainly between 109 and 116 degrees on July 6 through July 8 and on July 11 and 12. Afternoon highs were mainly between 104 and 110 degrees on July 2 through July 5 and on July 9, 10 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered offshore of southern Oregon and far northern California sent mid-level moisture northward and created an unstable air mass, leading to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the Shasta Valley city of Montague.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 MPH wind gust was reported at the Montague Airport with the passage of an outflow boundary from a nearby thunderstorm.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated shower and thunderstorm chances returned 20 July, and remained mostly south of Highway 50 with Mono-Mineral Counties being the focal points for activity. High-resolution ensemble guidance also showed the best chances for showers and thunderstorms mostly south of highway 50. Simulations projected a 30-40% chance for isolated showers and a few storms that would initiate mostly across Mono, southern Lyon, and Mineral counties around 1-2PM. There was also a chance (10-20%) that one or two storms would push northeast into eastern Churchill County as well. Storms quickly tapered off in the evening after sundown. Afternoon west-southwest breezes of 20-30 mph were expected to be slightly enhanced  in the vicinity of developing storms. The usual storm related culprits of copious lightning, as well as gusty and erratic outflows accompanied storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported mud, dirt , and rock around Lat/Lon: 38.388461, -119.452176 about 2.0 mi north of the Sonora junction of SR108/US-395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 20th brought locally heavy rain to portions of the Sierra Nevada. Caltrans Kyburz Maintenance responded to a mudslide near the Caldor Fire Burn Scar on Eastbound Highway 50 near Strawberry on this day. The slide did not impact traffic. No observations were available in the area but radar estimated rain amounts of around an inch in under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans Kyburz Maintenance responded to a mudslide near the Caldor Fire Burn Scar on Eastbound Highway 50 near Strawberry on July 20th, as thunderstorms were producing locally heavy rain over the mountains on this day. The slide did not impact traffic. No observations were available in the area but radar estimated rain amounts of around an inch in under an hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 108 and 113 degrees on July 23 and 24, and between 105 and 109 degrees on July 22, 25 and 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bogus Wildfire was discovered on July 8th at 302 PM PST. The fire started 14 miles north of Montague, near Ager, in Siskiyou County, California. The cause of the fire is undetermined. As of 935 PM PST on 07/15, it had burned 411 acres, was 100% contained, 2 structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $ 2M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge of high pressure pushes inland into California on July 2. This ridge provided our area with a hot and dry airmass which persisted for several days until the ridge moved east and temperatures cooled below dangerous levels on July 14. Several record high maximum temperatures and high minimum temperatures were set between July 6 and July 8 and again on July 11 and 12. In addition to the persistent heat between July 2 and July 13, low daytime humidity with poor overnight recovery and heavy fuel loading resulted in an increased fire danger for much of the area during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the 12 day heat wave event, afternoon highs were mainly between 109 and 116 degrees on July 6 through July 8 and on July 11 and 12. Afternoon highs were mainly between 104 and 110 degrees on July 2 through July 5 and on July 9, 10 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration heat event brought dangerously hot conditions with widespread Major to Extreme HeatRisk to interior Northern California over the first two weeks of July. Multiple temperature records were broken or tied over this timeframe. The Sacramento County Coroner reported two fatalities due to the extreme heat, one on July 6th and one on July 7th. Temperatures cooled slightly for a brief period in the Southern Sacramento Valley, Delta and Carquinez Straight, and Northern San Joaquin Valley on July 8th and 9th, but conditions were still hot, before ramping up again on July 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously hot daytime high temperatures of 100 to 118 degrees were observed from July 2nd through July 13th. Little relief from the heat was observed overnight, with low temperatures around 75 to 85 degrees over the same timeframe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure strengthened over central California on June 11 which resulted in daytime temperatures rising to the triple digits once again across most of the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills. Some cooling was noted along the west side of the valley on June 12 as an upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast although temperatures further inland remained well above normal. As the low moved inland on June 13, temperatures cooled further and remained below dangerous heat levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 100 and 104 degrees on June 11 and June 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Diego River reached 11.3 feet at USGS Fashion Mall gauge. Flooding in the area was significant including full closure and barricades with flowing water over all crossings, Qualcomm Way, Mission Center road, Fashion Valley road, Camino del Este, Ward road, and Mission road. San Diego fire reported 25 water rescues in the region and tributaries. The Fashion Valley parking garage had 2 to 3 feet of water in the lowest level. The Ward and Fashion Valley trolly centers were surrounded by 1 to 3 feet of water. Water was flowing up to the Riverleaf Inn, mostly blocked by K-rail but was flowing into Hotel circle. Flooded areas were observed as early as 1 pm. Flash flooding along the river including Ward road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed through the Pacific Northwest on June 1 and 2. This system produced increased onshore pressure gradients between the evening of June 1 and the morning of June 2 resulting in a period of gusty winds along the Mojave Desert slopes with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on June 16 following the system which had pushed through during the two previous days. This system brought another cold front which pushed through central California between the afternoon of June 16 and the morning of June 17. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts reported wind gusts exceeding 45 mph during this time frame with several stations along the Mojave Desert Slopes measuring gusts exceeding 65 mph. The strong winds continued through the early morning hours of June 18 before diminishing by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nomojw Mesonet station 2SSE Mojave measured a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on June 16 following the system which had pushed through during the two previous days. This system brought another cold front which pushed through central California between the afternoon of June 16 and the morning of June 17. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts reported wind gusts exceeding 45 mph during this time frame with several stations along the Mojave Desert Slopes measuring gusts exceeding 65 mph. The strong winds continued through the early morning hours of June 18 before diminishing by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cache Creek measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northern transport of moisture around the periphery of the ridge building over the Great Basin region, along with increased low level convergence ahead of an incoming upper trough, increased the showers and Thunderstorm chances across the Sierra from Mono County to the Lake Tahoe basin as well as east and north across western Nevada. The surge of monsoonal moisture pushed PWAT values upwards of 0.7-1.0 that were close to the 95th percentile for this time of the season. Storms were projected to have the potential for localized heavy rainfall in addition to gusty and erratic outflow winds, and lightning that raised new fire start concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol (CHP) located 12.0 mi ESE of Bodie, CA reported SR167 / SR359 closed from US395 to Hawthorne, NV due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remains of Atlantic Tropical Storm Alberto wrapped around high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest the pulled up north behind it into central California on June 24 and 25. The result of this was thunderstorms which had little to no precipitation with them on both days. One cluster of thunderstorms produced dry lightning which ignited several wildfires east of Reedley during the afternoon of June 24 which became known as the Fresno Lightning Complex Fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry lightning sparked several wildfires during the afternoon and evening of June 24 east of Reedley and Sanger. These fires became known as the Fresno Lightning Complex which resulted in several evacuations as the fires spread rapidly due to heavy fuel loading. The transmitter for KVPT Channel 18 was destroyed by one of the fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An adult male in his 30s went missing from a large party that was rafting down the American River toward Rossmoor Bar around 11:29 AM on Saturday, June 15th, 2024. The man was not wearing a life jacket and was swept underwater, last seen at Clay Banks. Cold and fast moving waterways were being observed due to snowmelt, twice the average flow for this time of year, and water temperatures in the upper 50s to low 60s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An adult male in his 30s went missing from a large party that was rafting down the American River toward Rossmoor Bar around 11:29 AM on Saturday, June 15th, 2024. The man was not wearing a life jacket and was swept underwater, last seen at Clay Banks. Numerous resources were called to search, including firefighters, a Sacramento Metro Fire helicopter and search party, Sacramento Drowning Accident Rescue Team (DART), and Sacramento County Sheriff's Office. His body was recovered in the morning, on Sunday, June 16th, 2024. Broadcast media reported that a sizable Sierra snowpack has led to river levels that are higher and faster than usual for this time of year, and that recent flows on the river at Fair Oaks were measured as fast as 3,600 cubic feet per second, twice the mean average for this time of year, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. Water temperature observations nearby showed temperatures in the upper 50s to low 60s around the general area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fatalities were reported due to cold and fast moving waterways along the North Fork American River near Auburn on June 25th, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crews responded to the report of a possible drowning at Lake Clementine Trail before 5:30 p.m on June 25th, 2024. A communications manager for California State Parks said three people had attempted to swim across the river and were swept downstream by the current. A 33-year-old man was found dead in the water, the El Dorado County Sheriff's Office said. Officials said a 15-year-old boy was found, and CPR was performed by State Parks lifeguards and Cal Fire. He was taken to Sutter Auburn Faith Hospital where he later died. State parks said another teenage male was pulled from the water and was uninjured. Water temperatures were in the low to mid 60's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge built into central California from the Desert Southwest on June 22 and remained over the region through June 25 before weakening ahead of an approaching trough. The ridging resulted widespread triple digit heat across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and lower Sierra foothills between June 22 and June 25. Some tropical moisture from the remains of Tropical Strom Alberto was pulled up by the upper high and spread into southern California on June 23 and into central California on June 24 which resulted in higher heat indexes across the lower elevations on June 24 and some dry thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening on June 24 which ignited several grass and brush fires in the eastern portion of the San Joaquin Valley and the Sierra foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 107 degrees on June 22, between 106 and 110 degrees on June 23 and between 101 and 105 degrees on June 24 and 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge built into central California from the Desert Southwest on June 22 and remained over the region through June 25 before weakening ahead of an approaching trough. The ridging resulted widespread triple digit heat across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and lower Sierra foothills between June 22 and June 25. Some tropical moisture from the remains of Tropical Strom Alberto was pulled up by the upper high and spread into southern California on June 23 and into central California on June 24 which resulted in higher heat indexes across the lower elevations on June 24 and some dry thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening on June 24 which ignited several grass and brush fires in the eastern portion of the San Joaquin Valley and the Sierra foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 107 degrees on June 22, between 106 and 110 degrees on June 23 and between 101 and 105 degrees on June 24 and 25.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several people were overcome by the heat at the American Canyon High School graduation. Three people were taken to the hospital and two more got medical attention on site. Temperatures in the area reached 91 degrees.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWESTERN SHASTA COUNTY TO WESTERN COLUSA COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty northerly winds and low relative humidity led to rapid fire spread for the Sites Fire and Aero Fires, on June 17th-18th, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sites Fire started on June 17th, 2024 in the afternoon and spread rapidly, especially within the first 24 hours due to strong and gusty northerly winds and low relative humidity. Relative humidity fell to the single digits on June 17th, and remained very dry through the 18th. The fire grew rapidly during these 2 days through grass and brush. Minimal growth was observed afterwards. The fire cause was under investigation, and was 100% contained on June 28th, with a final size of 19,195 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat from multiple days of hot daytime temperatures and very warm overnight temperatures was observed on June 4th through June 6th across much of interior Northern California. Daily maximum record temperatures were tied or broken, with Vacaville Airport reaching 104 degrees on June 5th, breaking the old record of 103 set in 2002. Marysville Airport reached 104 degrees on June 5th, which tied the old record of 104 set in 2002.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations in the area reported daytime high temperatures of 90 to 100 degrees across this zone, with overnight lows in the 60s to upper 70 degree range from June 4th through June 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures received via social media of flooding on roads in Ocean Beach, specifically on Spray Street near the dog beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the Pacific Northwest helped push a cold front through central California between the evening of June 14 and the morning of June 15 resulting a period of increased winds along the Mojave Desert Slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty northerly winds and low relative humidity led to rapid fire spread for the Sites Fire and Aero Fires, on June 17th-18th, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Aero Fire started in the afternoon on June 17th, 2024 near Rock Creek Road and Highway 4, near Copperopolis. The cause was under investigation, and spread over 5000 acres within the first 24 hours due to strong northerly winds and low relative humidity. Northerly winds were around 10 to 20 mph sustained with gusts of 25 to 35 mph were observed, with minimum relative humidity around 20 to 25 percent. The fire reached 5285 acres but was 100 percent contained by June 27th, 2024. 1 structure was damaged, and 3 were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on June 16 following the system which had pushed through during the two previous days. This system brought another cold front which pushed through central California between the afternoon of June 16 and the morning of June 17. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts reported wind gusts exceeding 45 mph during this time frame with several stations along the Mojave Desert Slopes measuring gusts exceeding 65 mph. The strong winds continued through the early morning hours of June 18 before diminishing by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Needles Fire started in the 900 block of Bush Street and moved north, jumping the Colorado River into Mohave Valley. The fire eventually burned 203 acres - 60 in CA and 143 in AZ - and destroyed one structure in CA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire began in Needles at 1215 PST, burning one structure, and jumped the Colorado River into Mohave Valley, AZ at 1340 PST/1440 MST. The fire was stopped at a total of 203 acres (60 acres in CA and 143 in AZ) at 1530 PST/1630 MST. There was no structural damage reported on the AZ side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Atlantic Tropical Storm Alberto surged north up the Gulf of California and into the Mojave Desert, fueling isolated thunderstorms. One storm produced high winds.

After a month of cooler than average for coastal and valleys the first week of June began to heat up. Palm Springs reached its first 110F, topping at 112F on June 6. With the warm up the live fuel moisture and dead fuels dried quickly especially across the mountains and deserts. The Relay fire erupted on June 6, and then the Carmen fire on June 11 in Riverside County. Just south of I-10, the Carmen fire burned 65 acres and destroyed 4 outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Springs airport first 110F, reaching 112F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat from multiple days of hot daytime temperatures and very warm overnight temperatures was observed on June 4th through June 6th across much of interior Northern California. Daily maximum record temperatures were tied or broken, with Vacaville Airport reaching 104 degrees on June 5th, breaking the old record of 103 set in 2002. Marysville Airport reached 104 degrees on June 5th, which tied the old record of 104 set in 2002.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations in the area reported daytime high temperatures of 90 to 100 degrees across this zone, with overnight lows in the 60s to low 70 degree range from June 4th through June 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In San Diego County, starting June 22, the Border 58 wildfire burned 137 acres east of Chula Vista on Otay Mountain Truck Trail, Otay Mountain Wilderness in Otay Mesa, California. Finally, in Del Mar Heights near Torrey Pines a wildfire burned 20 acres on June 25 and resulted in evacuation orders.  Riverside March ARB hit 106F on June 22 and  Palm Springs reached 115F again on June 23 and 24th, 2024, then the hottest date of the month was June 30 topping at 116F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hiker likely passed away from heat stroke off Nighthawk Trail on Black Mountain. Medical examiner is determining exact cause. At 700 am the temperature was 76F with a dewpoint 64F, wind southeast below 5 mph and relative humidity 67 percent at the base of Black Mountain and 93F at the summit of 1558 feet MSL. At 915 am the summit was 98F (SDHGPA site) and then the wind shifted to the west and gusted to 16 mph with a sea breeze lowering the temperature to 86F at the summit by 1100 am. At 1100 am, the base temperature was 84F and humidity at 58 percent. The highest temperature measured at the base was 86F at 1010 am per SDGE Rancho Penasquitos. Media reported a distress call to her family at 1008 am by the hiker saying she was very hot and she needed water. The hiker was separated from a large group of persons. The last contact to her sister was a photo from the top of Black Mountain summit. The hike is listed as moderate difficulty with 5.6 miles loop and roughly 3 hours to complete with a 850 feet elevation gain. Search and rescue consisted of 3 helicopters including infrared and drones as well as about 50 persons on foot. The search was called off at 1 am. Cell phone pings were not able to get an exact location. The search resumed at 730 am Monday. The 50 year old female was found at 915 am Monday just off the main trail and 1/4 mile from a population center. A heat advisory was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged and extended heat wave impacted the state of California from the early to the middle of July. An upper level ridge set up over the region, leading to a historically long period of dangerous heat. The event resulted in the issuing of an Excessive Heat Warning for interior regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, which extended From July 2 to the 10th and became the longest recorded Excessive Heat Warning issued by the NWS Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51-year old man drowned in the Russian River on Saturday July 6. A group of paddleboarders noticed a commotion near the Bohemian Highway Bridge around 6:40 PM and noticed the man in distress. They attempted CPR until fire officials arrived and attempted life saving measures for 25 minutes until he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged and extended heat wave impacted the state of California from the early to the middle of July. An upper level ridge set up over the region, leading to a historically long period of dangerous heat. The event resulted in the issuing of an Excessive Heat Warning for interior regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, which extended From July 2 to the 10th and became the longest recorded Excessive Heat Warning issued by the NWS Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 28-year old man drowned in the Russian River near the Patterson Point Preserve on Wednesday July 3, while seeking relief from the heat. Observers reported that the man was sucked down by a whirlpool around 6 PM and never came back on shore; his body was found near Monte Rio on Friday after a search.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenaya Lake SNOTEL (8150 feet) picked up an estimated 18 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system produced snow in the mountains and light rain across the coast and valleys. Several inches of snow were reported in the mountains, and thundersnow occurred in the Riverside County Mountains.
