EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold storm arrived in Southern California in early April. This supported a late season snow event at many local mountain communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm led to accumulating snow at many mountain communities in Southern California. Some notable snowfall totals include: 10 inches at Birch Hill on Palomar Mountain (5700') and 7 inches at Mount Laguna Lodge (6000').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave caused westerly/onshore winds to increase, becoming strong and gusty over the mountains and deserts of Southern California. Many of the typical wind-prone canyons and passes saw very high wind gusts occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass starting early in the afternoon on April 24th and lasted through the afternoon on April 26th. Notable peak wind gusts include: 75 mph at the Whitewater RAWS site, 60-65 mph at other sites near Whitewater, and 58 mph at Desert Hot Springs (SCE566).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved into central California on January 17 which resulted in clearing skies and light winds over the area by the evening. With low level moisture present in the San Joaquin Valley due to recent rainfall, dense fog formed overnight and continued through the late morning hours of January 18 until it lifted into a low cloud deck. The fog was responsible for a 6 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which resulted in one fatality and several injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree blocking the roadway 1S Oildale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough moved through central California on April 25 and 26. This system produced scattered showers across the area during the morning of April 26 and also brought a period of strong winds across the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County between the afternoon of April 25 and the evening of April 26 where several stations reported gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations across the remainder of the Kern County mountains and deserts reported gusts exceeding 45 mph during this same time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laural Mountain RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river impacted Southern California on February 1st. Impacts were felt across much of the region, but the most notable impacts were near the Seal Beach area. Several reports of flooding leading to road closures were received from this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across much of San Diego County on February 1st and 2nd. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.00-3.00 inches, with totals between 3.50-4.25 inches occurring in portions of the San Diego County Mountains near Palomar Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold storm arrived in Southern California in early April. This supported a late season snow event at many local mountain communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm led to accumulating snow at many mountain communities in Southern California. Some notable snowfall totals include: 14 inches at Snow Valley (7000-8000' in elevation), 14 inches at Bear Mountain Snow Summit (7000-8200'), 12 inches at all of Arrowbear Lake (6190'), Green Valley Lake (6900'), and Running Springs (6200'), 10.5 inches at both Barton Flats (6900') and Angelus Oaks (5780'), and 10 inches at Mount Baldy Notch (7800').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 32-foot long pleasure boat capsized near the Golden Gate Bridge on April 14. Coast Guard watchstanders received a notification at around 9:30 AM from a pilot boat captain that four boaters were in need of rescue after the boat capsized. A San Francisco Police Department boat crew and a pilot boat arrived to rescue the crew, and transferred them to a Coast Guard motor lifeboat, which brought them to Horseshoe Bay to be treated for hypothermia. At the time of the initial notification, the San Francisco Bar buoy (46237) reported significant waves of 1.8 m (around 6 feet) from 287 degrees, a dominant period of 12 seconds, and an average period of 8.1 seconds. Swell waves were 1.4 m (around 4.5 ft) at 11.8 seconds from the west-northwest, wind waves were 1.1 m (around 3.5 ft) at 9.5 seconds from the west-northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32-foot long pleasure boat capsized near the Golden Gate Bridge on April 14. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low that moved inland the afternoon and evening of the 18th brought increased rain, snow above 8500 feet, and thunder chances to the region on the 19th. Higher resolution ensemble simulations also continued to project chances for showers and isolated thunderstorms across the Sierra, the Sierra Front and vicinity as the upper low swung southeast towards southern Nevada. The best dynamics with this system remained mostly northward of the low pressure center with a jet streak located in the vicinity of the Eastern Sierra. High-Resolution Rapid Response guidance consistently showed concurrence with this scenario. Differences noted in ensemble simulations, however, lead to some uncertainty with the intensity of shower activity. Therefore, the expected intensities would range between light to moderate rainfall with isolated areas of heavy rainfall. Simulations showed that Mono County and the Eastern Sierra would have the best chances for higher elevation snowfall above 8500 with blended guidance showing a 20-50% chance for storm total snow greater than 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS (KTVL-South Lake Tahoe Airport) located 3.0 mi N of Meyers, CA,  and mesonet station EW3758 (Meyers) located 1.0 mi NNE of Meyers, CA, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.57 inches and 0.55 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire incident continued through the month of September, reaching 100% containment on September 26th. Cooler weather and recent rain from thunderstorm aided in suppressing fire activity along the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire began the month at 96% contained with 429,603 acres burned. No new structures were destroyed or damaged with minimal activity reported. Afternoon highs ranged from the upper 100s to mid 60s with minimum humidity as low as single digits. The fire was reported fully contained on September 26.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged and extended heat wave impacted the state of California from the early to the middle of July. An upper level ridge set up over the region, leading to a historically long period of dangerous heat. The event resulted in the issuing of an Excessive Heat Warning for interior regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, which extended from July 2 to the 10th and became the longest recorded Excessive Heat Warning issued by the NWS Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight heat-related deaths were recorded in Santa Clara County from July 7 to 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday September 7, a monsoonal easterly wind flow weather pattern set up and thunderstorms developed across the mountains and spread across the Inland Empire. Some of these storms had very heavy rainfall and prolific lightning. The actively burning Line fire was affected and helped develop additional persistent thunderstorms. On Sunday September 8, thunderstorms were most active in the Inland Empire where flash flooding and strong winds occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported flooding on I-10 causing closure of lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave developed starting Labor Day weekend and peaked the next weekend September 5 to 9, 2024. The hottest days included Ramona reaching 114, Santa Ana 113,  and Anaheim an all-time record of 112.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat started September 3 and continued through September 10, 2024. The hottest days were September 5 to 8, when high temperatures reached 105 to 114F across the entire Inland Empire. Despite scattered thunderstorms on September 7 and 8th, the high temperatures were still around 110F at many locations in the Inland Empire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public cameras showed a transition from hail to graupel (snow pellets) to dry snow over San Gorgonio mountain. This was also observed by dual pol radar algorithms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures across inland San Diego county valleys reached 100 to 112F from September 4 to 9, 2024. The hottest days were September 6 and 8, when maximum temperatures reached 103 to 110F along I-15 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season, storm brought significant rainfall to the area along with strong thunderstorms. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties to 1 to 2 inches across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Unstable air, associated with the storm, brought scattered strong thunderstorms to the area, producing gusty winds, large hail and even some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Porter Ranch area, a strong thunderstorm generated hail of one inch diameter.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge pushed into California from the eastern Pacific Ocean on September 3 and shifted inland and strengthened on September 5 and 6. This resulted in a period of much of above normal temperatures over the area with Heat Risk reaching high levels across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts. The ridge finally broke down and shifted east of the region on September 10 as a trough pushed into the Pacific Northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 102 and 107 degrees each day between September 4 and September 9.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 102 and 107 degrees each day between September 4 and September 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough that reached the west coast on the 10th brought dropped an attendant cold front southeast into western Nevada that increased west to southwest winds over the regional. Guidance projected winds to mix down into most valley areas with gusts peaking at 30-35 mph with a good chance for some wind prone areas seeing 50 mph. Higher elevation wind gusts peaked early in the day with peak ridge level wind gusts of 50-60 mph. This resulted in impacts for recreation, air/road travel, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) located 1.7 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit reported a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO COUNTY-UPPER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building overhead brought dangerously hot temperatures to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached on three straight days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Guidance showed an upper level low opening into a trough and passing over northern California into western Nevada during the evening hours the 2nd. The attendant cold front produced gusty southwest winds throughout the day into the evening. Ridgetop wind gusts were projected to increased to around 50-70 mph by the morning of the 2nd, and last through the day and overnight. These winds allowed for critical fire weather conditions, choppy waters on area lakes, aviation impacts, and blowing dust near desert areas. Higher resolution model guidance also suggested the potential for showers and thunderstorms along and east of US-95 during the afternoon hours of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.5 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low that moved inland the afternoon and evening of the 18th brought increased rain, snow above 8500 feet, and thunder chances to the region on the 19th. Higher resolution ensemble simulations also continued to project chances for showers and isolated thunderstorms across the Sierra, the Sierra Front and vicinity as the upper low swung southeast towards southern Nevada. The best dynamics with this system remained mostly northward of the low pressure center with a jet streak located in the vicinity of the Eastern Sierra. High-Resolution Rapid Response guidance consistently showed concurrence with this scenario. Differences noted in ensemble simulations, however, lead to some uncertainty with the intensity of shower activity. Therefore, the expected intensities would range between light to moderate rainfall with isolated areas of heavy rainfall. Simulations showed that Mono County and the Eastern Sierra would have the best chances for higher elevation snowfall above 8500 with blended guidance showing a 20-50% chance for storm total snow greater than 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRAHS station (CA-MN-8) located 1.2 mi NE of Walker, CA, and mesonet station WACL1(Walker) located 3.0 mi E of Coleville, CA, reported a total rainfall amount of 0.96 inches and 0.74 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon surge occurred on Sunday August 11, 2024. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across the San Diego mountains and into the Riverside County mountains. Thunderstorms produced hail, wind gusts and rainfall rates of 1 inch in 60 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lucky Five SDGE weather station recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 43 mph at 240 pm August 11, 2024.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge strengthened over California on October 1 and 2 then remained over the region through October 6. This resulted in several days of record heat over the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra Foothills. October 3 was the hottest day of the heat wave as several long standing all time record highs for the month of October were exceeded. The ridge finally shifted east into the Desert Southwest region on October 7 which reduced the heat impact across the area, but not before 3 additional days with  several records being broken. Temperatures finally cooled down to below record levels by October 10 as a weak shortwave trough moved into California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 101 and 106 degrees each day between October 2 and October 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on October 27 and pushed into the Pacific Northwest the following day. A cold front associated with the trough pushed through central California on October 28 bringing cooler temperatures and  light precipitation to the areas as well as a period of gusty winds to eastern Kern County where several stations measured wind guts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reported gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved into central California on October 16 bringing some light precipitation to the area between the afternoon of October 16 and the afternoon of October 17 along with a period of increased winds between the evening of October 16 and the morning of October 18. Precipitation amounts were fairly paltry with most of the area picking up trace amounts to a few hundredths of an inch although portions of Yosemite National Park picked up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level running around 9000 feet. This system also produced strong wind gusts along the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains as well as across the Mojave Desert Slopes in eastern Kern County. Several stations measured gusts in excess of 45 mph while a few low impact indicator stations measured gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Tom RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

Several stations measured gusts in excess of 45 mph while a few low impact indicator stations measured gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm ushered in cooler temperatures, as well as our initial round of showers, including snow showers across the highest terrain. A cold front that pushed across northern CA, and southern OR, early morning on the 15th spread the shower potential southward along the front with precipitation starting in western Nevada by mid-morning, and the eastern Sierra by the early afternoon. Showers persisted into the rest of central/eastern Nevada through the early evening. Blended guidance shows fairly high thunderstorm probabilities with 15-20% across the Basin and Range into western Nevada, and the highest chances (20-30%) across the Tahoe Basin and northeastern CA. Storm totals remained around 0.05-0.25 for much of the area, especially for northern Washoe and Lassen counties. Lower precipitation amounts (around 0.10 or less) were forecasted for areas south of I-80 in Mono and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (WWDC1-Westwood) located  4.0 mi SSW of Freonyer Pass, CA, a mesonet station (BUFC1-Bull Flat) located 11.0 mi NNE of Wendel, CA, a mesonet station (LAFC1-Laufman) located 6.0 mi S of Honey Lake, CA, and a trained spotter located 5.0 mi WNW of Susanville, CA, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.71 inches, 0.69 inches, 0.61 Inches, and 0.52 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were 2 skydivers in Lake Elsinore, one instructor and one recreationalist. While descending it was reported that a large dust devil extending over 100 feet above ground cause their parachute to twist and loose control. They accelerated to the ground. Both were pronounced dead, one at the scene and one at the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media outlets from LA reported 2 fatalities associated with skydiving deaths. At least 1 large dust devil was reported at the time of the accident and was reported to cause the instructor to loose control of the chute. The temperature was 103F and wind gusts were as high as 18 mph at nearby weather stations. This region is flat and know as the Inland Empire. The skydiving facility is at the Perris Valley airport. The instructor and the student died on scene and the hospital. Both skydivers were reported to be female and 28 years old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a heat wave dense fog from a very shallowed lowered marine layer occurred August 6 to 7, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Diego airport reported 1/4SM fog at 0600 August 7, 2024.  North Island Coronado reported 1/4SM fog at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge strengthened over California on October 1 and 2 then remained over the region through October 6. This resulted in several days of record heat over the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra Foothills. October 3 was the hottest day of the heat wave as several long standing all time record highs for the month of October were exceeded. The ridge finally shifted east into the Desert Southwest region on October 7 which reduced the heat impact across the area, but not before 3 additional days with  several records being broken. Temperatures finally cooled down to below record levels by October 10 as a weak shortwave trough moved into California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 101 and 106 degrees each day between October 2 and October 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought more impactful weather to the region starting on Sunday, the 27th, that continued into the beginning of the work week. Forecast guidance showed a Pacific Northwest trough making its way across western CONUS with the trough axis passing over the CWA on late Monday afternoon. This pattern aloft allowed a quick moving cold front to bring gusty southwest winds during the day on Sunday, 26th October, and wintry precipitation chances on that continued into through the early part of the week. The latest NBM probabilities for wind gusts of 40 mph or greater on the 26th showed the Lake Tahoe Basin having around a 40-60% chance of occurrence with the Reno area seeing around a 30-40% chance. A Lake Wind Advisory was therefore issued for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake for Sunday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.5 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain, a mesonet station (XMLC1-Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, and a mesonet station (565SE Sce-3577) located  4.0 mi NNE of Twin Lakes, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph, 62 mph, and 60 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low-pressure system moved into the western United States, it resulted in a tightening of the pressure gradient. As a result, strong westerly winds materialized across western portions of Imperial County, where isolated gusts in excess of 55 mph were observed during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Imperial County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of low relative humidity and breezy winds led to moderate spread of the Shoe Fire in Shasta County in October. The fire began in the afternoon of October 9th, and continued through the end of the month and into November. On October 31st, the fire was at 90% containment and 5,124 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low relative humidity and periods of breezy winds led to moderate spread of the Shoe Fire in October, located northeast of Shasta Lake. The fire began in the afternoon of October 9th, and continued through the end of the month and into November. On October 31st, the fire was at 5,124 acres and 90% contained. Evacuation orders were in place in the area, including road closures of Fenders Ferry Road at Pit River 7 Bridge, and Ferry Road at McCloud Bridge. There was 1 direct injury of a firefighter reported, due to heat combined with a 1 inch laceration to the wrist while responding to the fire during the first 24 hours of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the area behind a trough which moved through central California on October 12 and brought a cooler airmass to the area. The airmass also had higher humidity in the lower levels which had pushed into the area behind the trough. As skies cleared out during the morning of October 13, an area of dense fog formed toward sunrise along the SR 99 corridor between Selma and Tulare with webcams showing the lowest visibility in east Visalia. The fog lifted into stratus deck within two hours and completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Visalia. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Visalia and Tulare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This stronger of a one-two shot of storm systems advanced an east and south moving cold front inside slider-like mainly near and north of US-50. The bands of showers, and embedded thunder, also included very small hail as well. This front brought gusty northerly winds (30-40+ mph), choppy conditions especially for Pyramid Lake, and sharply colder temperatures. Shower and storm activity peaked during the mid-late afternoon, and then winded down as it moved south of US-50 by early this evening. Snow levels also dropped quickly to near lake level around the Lake Tahoe Basin early evening with a few snow showers remaining in the mix as well. Farther south into Mono County, a short period of upslope flow behind the front lead to minor snow accumulations near and west of US-395. The combination of the amplified northerly flow aloft, and persistent cold air advection allowed a few sites to receive up to 3 of additional lake-effect snowfall enhancement on larger lakes like Mono Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located at the Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows ski resort reported a peak wind gust of 101 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west northwesterly swell system with wave heights 7-10 feet at 16 - 18 seconds brings a moderate sneaker wave threat to northwest California. Large unexpected waves could surprise beachgoers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was swept into the Pacific Ocean near the visitor's center at Freshwater Lagoon, 1.5 miles south of Orick, CA. The lady's husband entered the water in an attempt to rescue his wife.  They both were caught in the waves and eventually rescued by other people on the beach using a human chain to pull them out of the water. The woman succumbed to her injuries. The man was in stable condition at the time of the incident and survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly 2 week heat wave occurred October 1 to October 12. Palm Springs reached 117F for a high on October 1, making it the hottest day recorded in October. It also reached 115F on October 6 and 7, 2024.  The month average high of 98.0F and average temperature of 83.3F (average high and low) making it the 3rd warmest on record. Hot temperatures also occurred across the inland valleys and mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Springs airport reached 117F on October 1st. Hottest day recorded in October. Note that Phoenix also recorded record highs of 117F in late September 28-29, 2024.

High temperatures in the Inland Empire, in region known as the Lake Elsinore convergence zone (most protected by sea breeze from the south and another from the northwest) reached 104 to 108F each day. This was when air temperature monitoring was occurring at Galway Downs equestrian center and brought high concern about hosting events in Temecula in the Los Angeles 2028 Olympics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brush fire occurred on Halloween in La Mesa near the San Diego State University campus and just south of I-8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brush fire burned 40 acres near San Diego State University at Montezuma Road and Fairmount Avenue. The wildfire changed names so there was some confusion on the name initially, Montezuma or Fairmount. 6 homes reported damaged per media at Lila Drive and Lucille Drive. Evacuation orders (mandatory) were issued. 300 firefighters were involved in the suppression. Relative humidity was only as low as 40 percent and winds were normal daily sea breeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passing to the north of region, tightened pressure and thermal gradient across the region that resulted in wind gusts up to 45 mph with higher gusts across higher terrain, especially up along the Oregon border. This created impacts to recreation, fire weather, and aviation. These conditions required issuance of a Red Flag Warning for NE-CA and NW-NV Friday, as well as issuance of Lake Wind Advisories for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake late morning through early evening Friday. Increased turbulence early Friday morning resulted in bumpy commercial flights locally to include potential for areas of blowing dust, and low visibilities, along Hwy 50, Hwy 95, and I-80 out east in the Basin and Range. This included a slight (10-15%) chance of rain showers Friday in Mono and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM) Blue Streak Weather station located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system diving down from the Gulf of Alaska brought isolated post-frontal high winds to the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak measured gust occurred 9 miles S of Lone Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of high pressure systems contributed to a week-long heat wave to begin the month of October. During this period, every long-term climate site in the region set or tied at least one daily high temperature record. In San Jose, a high of 106 recorded on October 2 set a monthly high temperature record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sonoma County reported 19 calls to 911 for heat related illnesses between October 2 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity developed over the San Diego Mountains during the afternoon hours on the 11th, likely instigated by large-scale forcing for ascent from an upper-level low located to the south. Outflows from the initial thunderstorm complex over the San Diego mountains helped to initialize new activity across western Imperial County. With PWAT values of around 2.0 inches, conditions were favorable for heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showing flash flooding in multiple spots along Split Mountain Rd, with most significant flooding at the Fish Creek Wash crossing. The road was washed out at this point and floodwaters were reportedly as deep as 4-6 feet based on sediment flow and depth of the wash. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity developed over the San Diego Mountains during the afternoon hours on the 11th, likely instigated by large-scale forcing for ascent from an upper-level low located to the south. Outflows from the initial thunderstorm complex over the San Diego mountains helped to initialize new activity across western Imperial County. With PWAT values of around 2.0 inches, conditions were favorable for heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-98 closed between Imperial Highway and Brockman Road due to flooding with several vehicles hydroplaning on the road. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon surge occurred on Sunday August 11, 2024. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across the San Diego mountains and into the Riverside County mountains. Thunderstorms produced hail, wind gusts and rainfall rates of 1 inch in 60 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Diego department of Public Works reported mud flows and flash flooding on the Great Southern Overland Stage route S2 and Vallecito Wash crossings. 2.54 inches of rain was recorded. Public Works also provided photos of the flooding and NWS did a storm survey the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire continued to burn into the month of August with a fire perimeter crossing Tehama and Butte Counties, north of Chico and east of Interstate 5. Slightly below normal temperatures and seasonal fire weather conditions allowed for limited additional spread of the fire by the end of the month. Evacuation warnings and orders ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire began the month at 22% contained with 393,012 acres. During the first half of the month, above normal temperatures and elevated fire weather conditions continued active spread and activity. Afternoon highs ranged from 82 to 104 with dry conditions of minimum humidities ranging from 10-25%. Rapid acre growth tapered off after August 8th, reaching 427,067 acres, 34% containment, 636 structures destroyed. ||Conditions cooled to normal to below normal temperatures with seasonal fire weather conditions, including scattered rain showers, allowing for lowered fire activity. Temperatures ranged from mid 70s to mid 90s with containment at 79% and total acres at 429,603. By the end of the month, 709 Structures have been destroyed with 54 damaged. Currently, minimal fire activity is reported, well within the fire perimeter. An IMET remains deployed to the fire and is tasked through mid September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two bodies were recovered over an early August weekend due to cold-water drownings. The first was found on 8/3 in the American River at the Clay Banks near Rancho Cordova. A man in his 30s went underwater and never resurfaced. He was not wearing a life jacket. The second male was found on 8/4 in the Sacramento River, 1 mile south of Garcia Bend in the Pocket neighborhood. He went underwater and never resurfaced. A third body was recovered on 8/6 on the Stanislaus River near Salida, CA. Emergency personnel responded to a report of a distressed person in the water. The body of a 26 year old male was found, and lifesaving measures were attempted but he was later pronounced deceased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 4th, a man's body was found in the Sacramento River, 1 mile south of Garcia Bend in the Pocket neighborhood. He went underwater and never resurfaced. The body was located by bystanders on a boat.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened over the Great Basin between August 4 and 6 bringing a return of dangerous heat to the area. The ridge strengthened further on August  7 resulting in several new daily climatological records being set before a cold front pushed through central California on August 8 which lowered temperatures below dangerous levels at most locations although widespread triple digit heat prevailed across the lower elevations through August 11. An upper trough moved into central California on August 12 lowering temperatures to near normal levels for the middle portion of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 104 and 109 degrees each day between August 4 and August 6 and between 107 and 112 degrees on August 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Crozier Fire started in the night on August 7th, 2024 northwest of Slate Mountain and burned actively for the first two days. The fire burned 1,960 acres and was reported 100% contained on August 22nd. Seasonably hot temperatures, low relative humidity, dry fuels, and locally breezy winds were observed during the initial spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crozier Fire started in the night on August 7th, 2024 and was discovered at 1:21 AM. It burned actively for the first two days in steep, rugged timber and in dry fuels. The fire burned a total of 1,960 acres and was reported 100% contained on August 20th.  Evacuation orders were in place for Garden Valley, Georgetown, Volcanoville, Mosquito, Quintette, and Swansboro areas. Evacuation warnings were in place for areas to the east and southwest of the fire. Seasonably hot temperatures, low relative humidity, and locally breezy wind gusts of up to 10-20 mph were observed during the initial spread. Daytime highs in the 90s to around 100 degrees were observed during the active burning periods, with daytime relative humidity in the low teens and poor overnight recoveries around 20-30%.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough slowly moved eastward across California on August 24 and brought gusty winds to portions of the Sierra Nevada and Kern County Desert areas. Several stations recorded gusts exceeding 45 mph during the afternoon and evening and August 24 with a few low impact indicator sites measuring gusts exceeding 55 mph. This system also brought cooler than normal temperatures to the area for a few days as well as some light precipitation. The trough moved east of the area during the morning of August 25 and winds decreased as the trough moved out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration high surf caused damage across Monterey Bay and resulted in numerous attempted water rescues on December 23. Around 1244 PST, around 150 feet of the Santa Cruz wharf collapsed and fell into Monterey Bay. Prior to 1300 PST, wave heights at buoy 46284 were building to around 18.7 feet with a dominant period of around 22 seconds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marina Police received a call around noon about a man in his 30s swept into the sea at the Marina State Beach at noon on December 23. The Monterey County Sheriff's Office called the Coast Guard for assistance, but they were unable to retrieve the body.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved through California on December 14 arriving during the late morning hours. The main impact that this system has it that a mid level jet stream ahead of it produced a period of strong wind gusts which mainly affected the West Side Hills and the Sierra Foothills in Mariposa and Madera Counties during the late morning and early afternoon of December 14. Several stations in these areas reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph with some stations picking up gusts in excess of 75 mph. Power outages were reported in several communities in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills due to the strong wind gusts knocking down trees and power lines. In addition, this system also produced moderate precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills from Fresno County northward with several stations picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation and several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Further south in the Sierra Nevada, precipitation was lighter with most locations receiving a tenth to a quarter of an inch of liquid precipitation. Rain shadowing limited most of the San Joaquin Valley to a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall. The trough moved to the east of the area by the evening of December 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Las Yeguas measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved eastward across northern California during the evening of December 26 and the morning of December 27 spreading precipitation southward into central California. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall by late morning of December 27 while areas from Fresno northward generally had a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while amounts|were less than a tenth of an inch south of Delano. Much of the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while SNOTELs indicated an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada above 6500 feet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Rocky Court measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm arrived on the 21st, and provided light rain, high elevation snow, and strong gusty winds. This later system appeared to be the most impactful as southwest to west winds approached 35-45 mph across lower elevation Sierra communities and the Sierra Front morning through the afternoon time period on the 21st with wind gusts exceeding 90 mph along exposed Sierra ridgetops. This storm system brought additional impacts to recreation, and brought bumpy flights into and out of regional airports. Rain and snow totals were relatively meager with this storm with accumulated snowfall of only 1-4 inches confined to elevations above 7000-7500 feet on the night of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM-Blue Streak Weather Station) located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski summit, mesonet station (RNYC1 Rice Canyon) located 5.0 mi SSE Sierraville, CA, and mesonet station (LIB03 Woodfords) located in Woodfords, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 77 mph, 73 mph, and 62 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger storm arrived Sierra and western Nevada during the night of 23rd that brought gusty winds, valley rain, and heavier mountain snowfall through Christmas Eve. Snowfall totals trended upwards, but chances of 6+ inches and 12+inches of snow along the Sierra crest by Christmas morning remained in excess of 70% and 40%, respectively. Meanwhile, there was a 20-30% chance that local foothills to include Virginia City would see at least a dusting of snow. Gusty southwest to west winds returned late on the 23rd into Christmas Eve with most locales having a 40% or greater chance for gusts reaching 30+ mph. As such, there remained high confidence that impacts to recreation and travel will unfold on Christmas Eve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (XMLC1 Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, and mesonet station (XMPC1 Mill Canyon) located 6.0 mi NNE of Leavitt Meadows, CA, reported peak wind gusts of 74 mph, and 62 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved eastward across northern California during the evening of December 26 and the morning of December 27 spreading precipitation southward into central California. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall by late morning of December 27 while areas from Fresno northward generally had a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while amounts|were less than a tenth of an inch south of Delano. Much of the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while SNOTELs indicated an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada above 6500 feet. This system also produced strong winds with widespread peak gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Mojave Desert Slopes and far south end of the Upper Sierra in Kern County during the morning of December 27. While the precipitation ended by late morning, the strong wind gusts continued through the evening before finally diminishing by the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cache Creek CHP station measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With low level moisture present from recent rains and mainly clear skies and light winds prevailing, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning of December 29. The fog lifted into a stratus deck then dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Chowchilla and Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of quick-moving shortwave troughs will across the Pacific NW the 26th and 27th that brought two waves in short succession of gusty winds and precipitation. The first round pushed across the region overnight Christmas through the morning of the 26th. After a brief break on the afternoon of the 26th, the second storm system passed through the area later in the night on the 26th into the 27th. The first wave brought light Sierra snow and valley rain with winds bringing the main impacts as a belt of amplified southwesterly wind aloft overspread the region. Ridgetop gusts exceeded  90 mph with lower valleys reaching 35-45 mph by the afternoon that induced impacts to recreation and travel. ||The bulk of the Sierra snow fell during the AM hour���s on the 26th with snow showers wrapping up that afternoon. Meanwhile, antecedent cold air from this  latest storm yield snow levels around 5000-5500 feet as showers arrive to the region. This increased the possibility of a mix of rain and snow for lower valley areas early on the 26th with snow levels rising to 6500-7000 feet that switched p-type to all rain. After a short window of dry weather Thursday afternoon, the second unstable wave brought impacts to the region overnight on the 26th into the 27th. Similar wind impacts occurred with this round of stormy weather favoring warmer and wetter conditions with higher snow levels (8000 feet) and heavier snowfall along the Sierra crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRGM-Mid-Station Gondola Weather Station) located 1.0 mi ENE of the Heavenly Ski summit, and mesonet station (TADC1-Tahoe-Donner) located 4.0 mi WNW of Truckee, CA,  reported peak wind gusts of 101 mph, and 74 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved through California on December 14 arriving during the late morning hours. The main impact that this system has it that a mid level jet stream ahead of it produced a period of strong wind gusts which mainly affected the West Side Hills and the Sierra Foothills in Mariposa and Madera Counties during the late morning and early afternoon of December 14. Several stations in these areas reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph with some stations picking up gusts in excess of 75 mph. Power outages were reported in several communities in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills due to the strong wind gusts knocking down trees and power lines. In addition, this system also produced moderate precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills from Fresno County northward with several stations picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation and several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Further south in the Sierra Nevada, precipitation was lighter with most locations receiving a tenth to a quarter of an inch of liquid precipitation. Rain shadowing limited most of the San Joaquin Valley to a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall. The trough moved to the east of the area by the evening of December 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite 49 measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong swell waves in the eastern Pacific resulted in two fatal boating accidents in the waters off the North Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hiker called 911 at 7 AM on Monday and reported that they had seen a boat capsize off Mussel Point and Bodega Head with two people onboard. The boat was 20 feet long and manufactured by Boston Whaler. A man was found dead on Bodega Dunes Beach at 7:40 AM. His companion remains missing. ||A Small Craft Advisory was in effect for the waters off Bodega bay at the time of the advisory. Mean wave direction was WNW with heights ranging between 8 and 12 feet at the Bodega Bay Buoy (Station 46013) during the pre-dawn hours on Monday (11/4). NNW to NW winds ranged between 13 and 19 knots with peak gusts around 25 knots during the pre-dawn hours on Monday (11/4).

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person died in the Sierra Nevada, presumably of cold-related causes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man attempted to climb Mt. Whitney solo despite having neither proper gear nor Alpine climbing experience. His body was found days later. It is presumed that he died of cold-related causes on the first day of his attempt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved through California on December 14 arriving during the late morning hours. The main impact that this system has it that a mid level jet stream ahead of it produced a period of strong wind gusts which mainly affected the West Side Hills and the Sierra Foothills in Mariposa and Madera Counties during the late morning and early afternoon of December 14. Several stations in these areas reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph with some stations picking up gusts in excess of 75 mph. Power outages were reported in several communities in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills due to the strong wind gusts knocking down trees and power lines. In addition, this system also produced moderate precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills from Fresno County northward with several stations picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation and several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Further south in the Sierra Nevada, precipitation was lighter with most locations receiving a tenth to a quarter of an inch of liquid precipitation. Rain shadowing limited most of the San Joaquin Valley to a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall. The trough moved to the east of the area by the evening of December 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jerseydale RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough pushed across California on December 24 producing a period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation as well as a period of strong winds during the late morning and early afternoon. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up a quarter to half an inch of rainfall during the day which resulted in a few reports of nuisance flooding. Much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between four tenths and eight tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation with snow above 6500 feet. Several SNOTELs in the higher Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Eastern Kern County was mainly impacted by strong winds as several stations reported gusts exceeding 50 mph. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between two and four tenths of an inch of rainfall while the Deserts picked up a tenth of an inch or less due to rain shadowing. The trough moved to the east of the area by late afternoon and winds diminished during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving into the west coast brought heavy snow to elevations above 4000 feet in northern California. A secondary, trailing disturbance brought an additional, lighter snow accumulation during the day of 12/14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 6 to 12 inches at elevations between 4000 and 6000 feet, with up to 24 inches over the higher terrain. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.42 inches at Long Prairie. During the afternoon and evening of 12/13, peak wind gusts were recorded of 65 mph at Van Bremmer and 67 mph at Pluto's Cave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper trough dug south and east into the western US, an attendant cold front swung across northern CA early morning on the 29th and  continued across western NV later that morning through the afternoon. This last in a series of AR supported storm systems brought higher elevation snow, valley rain showers, and strong southwesterly winds to the region. This round of strong southwest winds included gusts up to 40-50 mph in western NV valleys, as well as 120+ mph wind across Sierra ridges. Wind prone areas also saw a 40-60% chance for peak gusts around 60+ mph. ||Impacts included hazardous driving conditions, especially for high profile vehicles, frequent periods of mountain wave turbulence, rotors, and LLWS for aviation concerns, choppy lake conditions on larger area lakes, and periods of blowing dust in dry lake beds/playas for the Basin and Range. Other potential impacts included downed trees, powerlines, and power outages. Blended guidance projected a 40-60% chance for up to 4-8 inches of snowfall at higher passes such as Donner Summit, Echo and Carson Pass. The gusty southwest winds and periods of moderate snow created additional travel challenges over higher mountain passes before tapering off overnight on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRGM-Mid-Station Gondola Weather Station) located 1.0 mi ENE of the Heavenly Ski summit reported a peak wind gust 91 mph.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved eastward across northern California during the evening of December 26 and the morning of December 27 spreading precipitation southward into central California. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall by late morning of December 27 while areas from Fresno northward generally had a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while amounts|were less than a tenth of an inch south of Delano. Much of the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while SNOTELs indicated an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada above 6500 feet. This system also produced strong winds with widespread peak gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Mojave Desert Slopes and far south end of the Upper Sierra in Kern County during the morning of December 27. While the precipitation ended by late morning, the strong wind gusts continued through the evening before finally diminishing by the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December 18 to 20, 2024, dense fog occurred along the coast and a few miles inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KSAN airport had 300 delays and 40 cancelled flights on Saturday December 21st. This was reported on broadcast media and Flight Aware.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December 18 to 20, 2024, dense fog occurred along the coast and a few miles inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog occurred from 430 pm December 18 to 830 am December 19, 2024 at San Diego International Airport (Lindbergh Field).  Dense fog returned 8 pm December 19 to December 20 at 7 am. On Friday December 20, 2024 there were 31 diversions and 201 cancelled flights at KSAN.  This was major news due to holiday week travelers of 1.3 million.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent high pressure ridge which maintained dry conditions over central California for the previous ten days finally broke down during the evening of December 11 as an upper low moved into northwestern California. This system spread widespread precipitation into the area although amounts were generally light with much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County and the Sierra Foothills picking up between one tenth and three tenths of an inch of rain with moderate rain being produced by a few thunderstorms around sunrise on December 12. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall between the early morning of December 12 and the evening of December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent high pressure ridge which maintained dry conditions over central California for the previous ten days finally broke down during the evening of December 11 as an upper low moved into northwestern California. This system spread widespread precipitation into the area although amounts were generally light with much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County and the Sierra Foothills picking up between one tenth and three tenths of an inch of rain with moderate rain being produced by a few thunderstorms around sunrise on December 12. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall between the early morning of December 12 and the evening of December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved through California on December 14 arriving during the late morning hours. The main impact that this system has it that a mid level jet stream ahead of it produced a period of strong wind gusts which mainly affected the West Side Hills and the Sierra Foothills in Mariposa and Madera Counties during the late morning and early afternoon of December 14. Several stations in these areas reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph with some stations picking up gusts in excess of 75 mph. Power outages were reported in several communities in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills due to the strong wind gusts knocking down trees and power lines. In addition, this system also produced moderate precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills from Fresno County northward with several stations picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation and several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Further south in the Sierra Nevada, precipitation was lighter with most locations receiving a tenth to a quarter of an inch of liquid precipitation. Rain shadowing limited most of the San Joaquin Valley to a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall. The trough moved to the east of the area by the evening of December 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved eastward across northern California during the evening of December 26 and the morning of December 27 spreading precipitation southward into central California. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall by late morning of December 27 while areas from Fresno northward generally had a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while amounts|were less than a tenth of an inch south of Delano. Much of the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while SNOTELs indicated an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada above 6500 feet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief lull on the 15th, this third in the ongoing trio of storm systems pushed quickly through the region on the 16th and early on the 17th. This storm system reached northeast California early morning on the 16th, impacted the Tahoe Basin by mid-late morning through the afternoon of that same date, and diminished as it tracked south into Mono County that evening. The primary impact with this storm were the gusty winds that increased to 80 mph over Sierra with projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the lower elevation Sierra valleys that mixed down into western Nevada. Snowfall was limited, however, due to its speed and timing during mostly daylight hours. But bursts of moderate to heavy snowfall with rates ranging 0.5-1.0 inch per hour between Lassen County south into the Tahoe Basin. Most snow impacts were concentrated over mountain passes as snow levels started at around 5000 to 6000 feet, then increased to 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows summit, CA,  and Mesonet station (HVRGM-Mid-Station Gondola Weather Station) located 1.0 mi ENE of the Heavenly Ski summit reported peak wind gusts of 156 mph, and 69 mph respectively.

Rain shadowing limited most of the San Joaquin Valley to a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall. The trough moved to the east of the area by the evening of December 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Panoche Road RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough pushed across California on December 24 producing a period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation as well as a period of strong winds during the late morning and early afternoon. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up a quarter to half an inch of rainfall during the day which resulted in a few reports of nuisance flooding. Much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between four tenths and eight tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation with snow above 6500 feet. Several SNOTELs in the higher Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. Eastern Kern County was mainly impacted by strong winds as several stations reported gusts exceeding 50 mph. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between two and four tenths of an inch of rainfall while the Deserts picked up a tenth of an inch or less due to rain shadowing. The trough moved to the east of the area by late afternoon and winds diminished during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree blocking both lanes of Auberry Rd. near the intersection of Spearhead Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved eastward across northern California during the evening of December 26 and the morning of December 27 spreading precipitation southward into central California. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall by late morning of December 27 while areas from Fresno northward generally had a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while amounts|were less than a tenth of an inch south of Delano. Much of the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while SNOTELs indicated an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada above 6500 feet. This system also produced strong winds with widespread peak gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Mojave Desert Slopes and far south end of the Upper Sierra in Kern County during the morning of December 27. While the precipitation ended by late morning, the strong wind gusts continued through the evening before finally diminishing by the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm arrived on the 21st, and provided light rain, high elevation snow, and strong gusty winds. This later system appeared to be the most impactful as southwest to west winds approached 35-45 mph across lower elevation Sierra communities and the Sierra Front morning through the afternoon time period on the 21st with wind gusts exceeding 90 mph along exposed Sierra ridgetops. This storm system brought additional impacts to recreation, and brought bumpy flights into and out of regional airports. Rain and snow totals were relatively meager with this storm with accumulated snowfall of only 1-4 inches confined to elevations above 7000-7500 feet on the night of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) located 1.7 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, and  mesonet station (XMPC1 Mean Peak) located 6.0 mi NNE of Leavitt Meadows, CA, reported peak wind gusts of 80 mph, and 76 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure remained prevalent over California on December 11 resulting in the continuation of mainly clear skies, light winds and inversion conditions over the San Joaquin Valley. This resulted in areas of dense fog forming over the center portion of the valley by sunrise reducing visibility to an eighth of a mile or less in several locations. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Hanford. Several school districts either opened late or delayed or cancelled bus service due to low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system came through the Bay Area and Central Coast through the night of Friday December 13 into Saturday December 14 as a cold front combined with a developing low pressure system off the California coast, causing numerous weather impacts throughout the region. Among the notable impacts from the system, a confirmed EF1 tornado touched down in Scotts Valley, the first recorded tornado warning in San Francisco received significant public and media attention, strong gusts associated with a squall line caused multiple weather impacts across the region, and most of the city of Monterey and the surrounding cities of Seaside and Marina lost power on Saturday morning and did not get it back until the following Sunday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado warning for San Francisco and San Mateo Counties was issued at 5:51 AM after a rotating thunderstorm was observed off the coast of San Francisco. This is the first tornado warning issued on record for the city of San Francisco, using records that extend to at least 2005 and potentially since 1986. After an NWS storm survey that extended from the west side of Golden Gate Park to the Richmond district, it was determined that damage along the path of the cell was consistent with straight line wind gusts around 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 19 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 19 along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise resulting in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service on December 20. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Hanford. Several school districts either opened late or delayed or cancelled bus service due to low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure center moved eastward across northern California on November 11. A cold front that was associated with the upper low pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening producing widespread wind gusts above 45 mph as well as areas of blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero in the San Joaquin Valley. The dust resulted in a 20 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which led to SR 152 being closed for much of the afternoon. Many stations measured between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with this system with several stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet with several stations in the higher Sierra Nevada picking up between 2 and 5 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation ended during the evening of November 11 and winds diminished during the early morning of November 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system which had remained nearly stationary off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days moved inland across the Pacific Northwest on November 26. This low pulled up a surge of deep moisture into central California between the evening of November 25 and the evening of November 26. This atmospheric river type system brought moderate to locally heavy precipitation to much of the area with most of the San Joaquin Valley picking up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Foothills and the Kern River Valley picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 7000 feet picking up an estimated 1 to 3 feet of new snow. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing limited precipitation in the Kern County Desert areas. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement in Fresno, Tulare and Kern Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported several rocks on SR 178 11SW Lake Isabella.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system came through the Bay Area and Central Coast through the night of Friday December 13 into Saturday December 14 as a cold front combined with a developing low pressure system off the California coast, causing numerous weather impacts throughout the region. Among the notable impacts from the system, a confirmed EF1 tornado touched down in Scotts Valley, the first recorded tornado warning in San Francisco received significant public and media attention, strong gusts associated with a squall line caused multiple weather impacts across the region, and most of the city of Monterey and the surrounding cities of Seaside and Marina lost power on Saturday morning and did not get it back until the following Sunday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Special Weather Statement was issued at 1:07 PM, followed by a Severe Thunderstorm Warning at 1:25 PM, for a strong thunderstorm that impacted western Santa Cruz County. Following the impact, social media videos and reports indicated that a tornado briefly touched down in the Scotts Valley region. A team from the National Weather Service San Francisco Bay Area conducted a storm survey in Scotts Valley. The team identified a swath of tornadic damage that began near the intersection of Mount Hermon Rd and Lockewood Ln and continued southeast along Mount Hermon Rd until just short of Kings Village Rd. A wide range of damage was observed including: downed trees, downed power poles, trees stripped of branches, numerous overturned vehicles, and damaged street signs.  Based on these damage indicators the survey team assessed a maximum wind speed of 90 mph, which is a weak EF1 tornado. Five people, including a firefighter, were injured by the tornado, and six cars were turned over including a pickup truck from Cal Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This first in a trio of storm systems began to impact the Sierra the night of the 11th, and continued through the morning of the 12th. Although not a major storm system, it produced travel impacts due to periods of moderate to heavy snowfall as it delivered a quick shot of snowfall across the Sierra with snow levels hanging in the 4000-5000 feet range. Accumulations of 8-10 inches on Sierra passes complemented a 4-6 hour window with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour before tapering off by mid-morning on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) located 1.7 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, mesonet station (XMPC1 Mean Peak) located 6.0 mi NNE of Leavitt Meadows, CA, and mesonet station (GW0353-Crowley Lake) located 2.0 mi W of Toms Place, CA,  reported peak wind gusts of 100 mph, 73 mph, and 68 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second warmer, and wetter, in a trio of modest storm systems began to impact the Sierra the afternoon of the 13th, and continued through the 14th. It produced its share of travel impacts with periods of heavy snowfall with snowfall accumulation of 15-20��� through the evening of the 14th. Although there was elevated uncertainty, lower elevation Sierra communities were impacted by 3-6 inches of snow in some areas. An increasing trend for enhanced precipitation shadowing favored the development of stronger winds across eastern Sierra into western Nevada.  Overall odds pointed toward a 60% chance for winds gusting in excess of 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA, and the June Lake Ski Base, CA, Main Lodge, reported a 24 hour snowfall amounts of 19.0 inches and 15.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty north to west winds developed on November 3rd, and brought down a tree at a flea market in Stockton, CA. Two injured men were taken to an area hospital as a result of this fallen tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delta College Police Department reported around 10:15 AM that the tree had fallen on two tents at a flea market. Two injured men were taken to an area hospital. Stockton Airport reported north to west sustained winds of 10 to 25 MPH with gusts of 15 to 35 MPH from 7 AM to 7 PM on Sunday. Around the time of the reported event, north to west winds were reported, sustained at 16 to 20 MPH, and gusting to 22 to 29 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A paddle boarder went missing in the San Pablo Bay off the coast of China Camp. The paddle boarder was last seen on December 14 wearing a black wetsuit and black beanie. The Coast Guard found his paddleboard on the morning of December 16 along the shoreline of Contra Costa County near Point Pinole. While there are few observations from the San Pablo Bay itself, nearby weather stations recorded a maximum gust of around 40-50 mph on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the storm that produced widespread precipitation across the area on December 24, skies cleared out on Christmas Eve and areas of dense fog formed along the SR 99 corridor in the San Joaquin Valley reducing visibility to an eighth of a mile for a few hours. Visibility improved by late morning as the fog lifted into a low cloud deck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Fresno. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Fresno and Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure center moved eastward across northern California on November 11. A cold front that was associated with the upper low pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening producing widespread wind gusts above 45 mph as well as areas of blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero in the San Joaquin Valley. The dust resulted in a 20 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which led to SR 152 being closed for much of the afternoon. Many stations measured between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with this system with several stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet with several stations in the higher Sierra Nevada picking up between 2 and 5 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation ended during the evening of November 11 and winds diminished during the early morning of November 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison at Fish Tail measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system which had remained nearly stationary off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days moved inland across the Pacific Northwest on November 26. This low pulled up a surge of deep moisture into central California between the evening of November 25 and the evening of November 26. This atmospheric river type system brought moderate to locally heavy precipitation to much of the area with most of the San Joaquin Valley picking up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Foothills and the Kern River Valley picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 7000 feet picking up an estimated 1 to 3 feet of new snow. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing limited precipitation in the Kern County Desert areas. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement in Fresno, Tulare and Kern Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a rock slide blocking both lanes on Trimmer Springs Rd. at Big Creek Rd. near Pine Flat Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 2. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning of December 3 which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile at several locations. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 5 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 5 along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise resulting in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service on December 6. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Merced and Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first atmospheric river of the winter storm season created flooding across the North Bay between November 20 and 23. The atmospheric river was accompanied by a very rapidly intensifying cyclone that deepened by 66 millibars within 24 hours, easily meeting the criteria for bomb cyclogenesis. The greatest impacts were concentrated across the North Bay as the river stalled over the region. The North Bay valleys received nearly a foot of rain in three days and the mountains received as much as 22.57 inches (from the Venado gauge).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding on 580 W and Ashby Avenue offramp. Water is up to door panel on vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weather system brought continued periods of heavy mountain snow with mountain travel delays and chain restrictions, widespread rain with flooding impacts, and gusty winds in the from November 22nd through November 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Placer County Sheriff reported a group of three men went to fish in the North American River, north of Folsom Lake on November 23rd. Two of the men, both 20 years old, got into the water. One of them was swept away by the strong currents and the second man tried to help but was also pulled into the current. The recovery operation was extremely technical and in a portion of the river with rapids and strong current due to the storm. Both 20 year old men drowned and their bodies were recovered downstream on November 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming system approaching northern California brought increased high clouds and mixing to central California during the morning of December 26 which inhibited widespread fog development. As a result, fog coverage was localized and the only reports of dense fog were near Lemoore where visibility was briefly reduced to an eight of a mile before the fog lifted into a low cloud deck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Lemoore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out and winds diminished across the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 27. With an additional supply of low level moisture from the rainfall from the morning of December 27 areas of fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 28 and reduced visibility to as low as an eighth of a mile. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and eventually dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced and visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Madera. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Merced, Chowchilla and Madera.
