EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through central California between the evening of November 14 and the evening of November 15. This system did bring widespread light precipitation to the area with the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, the Grapevine area and the Tehachapi area receiving the most significant precipitation between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Nevada picked up between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation with several SNOTEL stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up one to two inches of new snowfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert Slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

With an additional supply of low level moisture from the rainfall from the morning of December 27 areas of fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 28 and reduced visibility to as low as an eighth of a mile. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and eventually dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Fresno. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Fresno and Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather brought periods of heavy mountain snow, rain and gusty winds from December 12th through December 17th as multiple storms moved through the area. The strongest storm moved through over the December 13th through 14th timeframe and brought over 2 to 3 inches of rain, over 2 feet of heavy snow above 5000 feet, and gusty southerly wind gusts of 35 to 65 mph. Numerous downed trees and thousands of power outages were reported during this time, along with mountain highway closures and chain restrictions, with roadway flooding in the Central Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby CoCoRaHS at similar elevations measured snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated snow amounts of 2 to 8 inches from December 13th through the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through central California between the evening of November 14 and the evening of November 15. This system did bring widespread light precipitation to the area with the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, the Grapevine area and the Tehachapi area receiving the most significant precipitation between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Nevada picked up between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation with several SNOTEL stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up one to two inches of new snowfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert Slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that the exit 165 on ramp to westbound SR 58 was closed due to winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass pushed into central California on November 16. With clear skies and light winds prevailing the following night, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley for a short time during the early morning of November 17. It was also the first significant freeze of the season for most of the Kern County Desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system which had remained nearly stationary off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days moved inland across the Pacific Northwest on November 26. This low pulled up a surge of deep moisture into central California between the evening of November 25 and the evening of November 26. This atmospheric river type system brought moderate to locally heavy precipitation to much of the area with most of the San Joaquin Valley picking up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Foothills and the Kern River Valley picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 7000 feet picking up an estimated 1 to 3 feet of new snow. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing limited precipitation in the Kern County Desert areas. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement in Fresno, Tulare and Kern Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Devil's Postpile (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 22 inches of new snow. Green Mountain (7900 feet) picked up an estimated 19 inches of new snow. Chilkoot Meadow (7150 feet) picked up an estimated 14 inches of new snow. Graveyard Meadows (6897 feet) picked up an estimated 15 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 4 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 4 along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise resulting in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service on December 5. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the San Joaquin Valley following a cold frontal passage during the evening of December 29. Once winds diminished overnight, some patchy dense fog formed in the Joaquin Valley towards daybreak on December 30; but the fog not last long. A more noticeable impact was fog resulting from upslope winds over Interstate 5 near Tejon Pass and on SR 58 near Tehachapi Pass where fog impacted travel for a few hours during the afternoon and early evening of December 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first atmospheric river of the winter storm season created flooding across the North Bay between November 20 and 23. The atmospheric river was accompanied by a very rapidly intensifying cyclone that deepened by 66 millibars within 24 hours, easily meeting the criteria for bomb cyclogenesis. The greatest impacts were concentrated across the North Bay as the river stalled over the region. The North Bay valleys received nearly a foot of rain in three days and the mountains received as much as 22.57 inches (from the Venado gauge).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was blocking lanes on westbound Interstate 580 at Central Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant low level moisture in the area as a result of widespread precipitation over the area on November 25 and 26 combined with clearing skies and light winds for areas of dense fog to form during the evening of November 27 and early morning of November 28. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 in much of the valley including through Fresno and Bakersfield. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning on November 28 with improving visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Merced and Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 3 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 3 along portions of the SR 99 corridor which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise resulting in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service on December 4. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first atmospheric river of the winter storm season created flooding across the North Bay between November 20 and 23. The atmospheric river was accompanied by a very rapidly intensifying cyclone that deepened by 66 millibars within 24 hours, easily meeting the criteria for bomb cyclogenesis. The greatest impacts were concentrated across the North Bay as the river stalled over the region. The North Bay valleys received nearly a foot of rain in three days and the mountains received as much as 22.57 inches (from the Venado gauge).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported on all lanes of Westbound State Highway 24 at Camino Pablo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather brought periods of heavy mountain snow, rain and gusty winds from December 12th through December 17th as multiple storms moved through the area. The strongest storm moved through over the December 13th through 14th timeframe and brought over 2 to 3 inches of rain, over 2 feet of heavy snow above 5000 feet, and gusty southerly wind gusts of 35 to 65 mph. Numerous downed trees and thousands of power outages were reported during this time, along with mountain highway closures and chain restrictions, with roadway flooding in the Central Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree down on powerlines from gusty southerly winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture deficient trough moved through central California on November 18. This system did not have much precipitation with it as some stations from Fresno County northward picked up a tenth of an inch or less of liquid precipitation. However, the system did produce a period of increased winds as a result of a strong pressure gradient during the afternoon and evening of November 18 over mountains and deserts of Kern County. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact stations had gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Cache Creek measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a trough through central California on November 19 which brought clouds to the area resulting in temperatures remaining above freezing throughout the San Joaquin Valley on November 18, clearing skies and diminishing winds in a prevailing dry airmass resulted in subfreezing temperatures across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 19. The airmass moderated some by the following morning as a more moderate zonal flow pattern prevailed and temperatures remained above the freezing mark across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system moved through northern California on December 16 which brought some light precipitation to the area mainly to the north of Fresno County. An upper ridge shifted inland into California during the morning of December 17 behind this system which brought clearing skies and light winds to the San Joaquin Valley. This allowed for patchy dense fog to form in the center portion of the valley around sunrise on December 17. At least one multiple vehicle accident took place on SR 198 when visibility was reduced to an eighth of a mile. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed on the 20th with the approach of an upper level storm system that hovered off the Pacific NW. This allowed for a deep plume of moisture, aka atmospheric river (AR), to be advected towards northern CA for the second half of the week. This enhanced the primary hazard being heavy precipitation across the region with projected liquid storm totals of 2.5-3.0 inches for the Sierra crest, 0.75-1.00 inch for the foothills, and 0.10-0.75 from the Basin and Range into far western Nevada. The secondary hazard that was expected with this system was gusty southwest to south winds. Projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the valleys across the region were complemented with gusts exceeding 70-80 mph across the Sierra crest. Blended guidance showed a 30-50% chance of 50+ mph gusts, while ECMWF Extreme Forecast Index (EFI) also indicated an anomalous event. Other wind impacts included turbulence, hazardous boating conditions, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (RNYC1-Rice Canyon) located 5.0  mi SSE of Sierraville, CA reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

The fog reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile in a few locations. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Visalia and visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 7 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 7 along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise on December 8. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Fresno. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Fresno and Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system moved through northern California on December 16 which brought some light precipitation to the area mainly to the north of Fresno County. An upper ridge shifted inland into California during the morning of December 17 behind this system which brought clearing skies and light winds to the San Joaquin Valley. This allowed for patchy dense fog to form in the center portion of the valley around sunrise on December 17. At least one multiple vehicle accident took place on SR 198 when visibility was reduced to an eighth of a mile. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a multiple car accident in dense fog with visibility near an eighth of a mile involving three vehicles (2 cars and a tractor trailer) at the intersection of SR 198 and 6th Ave. east of Hanford. This accident was also confirmed onsite by an NWS employee. At least one person was injured in the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first atmospheric river of the winter storm season created flooding across the North Bay between November 20 and 23. The atmospheric river was accompanied by a very rapidly intensifying cyclone that deepened by 66 millibars within 24 hours, easily meeting the criteria for bomb cyclogenesis. The greatest impacts were concentrated across the North Bay as the river stalled over the region. The North Bay valleys received nearly a foot of rain in three days and the mountains received as much as 22.57 inches (from the Venado gauge).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landslide reported as completely blocking the southbound lanes of State Route 29 and Old Lawley Toll Road. About 6 to 8 feet of debris was reported on right hand side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of precipitation brought periods of heavy rain, mountain snow and gusty winds to interior Northern California over the Christmas holiday and through the end of the month. Storm total snow amounts of 10 to 40 inches were measured in the northern Sierra Nevada and southern Cascades from December 24th through 29th that led to periods of chain restrictions along the mountain passes. Additionally, there were multiple reports of roadway flooding in the Valley, and mud/dirt/rock slides in the foothills that led to brief road closures lasting a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding from heavy rain on State Route 36 near Mile Marker 49.4.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a trough through central California on November 19 which brought clouds to the area resulting in temperatures remaining above freezing throughout the San Joaquin Valley on November 18, clearing skies and diminishing winds in a prevailing dry airmass resulted in subfreezing temperatures across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 19. The airmass moderated some by the following morning as a more moderate zonal flow pattern prevailed and temperatures remained above the freezing mark across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High clouds streaming over the area as a result of an offshore upper low pressure system inhibited fog from forming in the San Joaquin Valley until well into the morning hours of November 30 although once the fog developed, visibility dropped below a quarter mile across much of the south San Joaquin Valley. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet along portions of Interstate 5 west of Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed on the 20th with the approach of an upper level storm system that hovered off the Pacific NW. This allowed for a deep plume of moisture, aka atmospheric river (AR), to be advected towards northern CA for the second half of the week. This enhanced the primary hazard being heavy precipitation across the region with projected liquid storm totals of 2.5-3.0 inches for the Sierra crest, 0.75-1.00 inch for the foothills, and 0.10-0.75 from the Basin and Range into far western Nevada. The secondary hazard that was expected with this system was gusty southwest to south winds. Projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the valleys across the region were complemented with gusts exceeding 70-80 mph across the Sierra crest. Blended guidance showed a 30-50% chance of 50+ mph gusts, while ECMWF Extreme Forecast Index (EFI) also indicated an anomalous event. Other wind impacts included turbulence, hazardous boating conditions, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (448SE Sce-3406) located 4.0  mi SSE of Conway Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on November 1 and into the Pacific Northwest on November 2 then dropped into the Great Basin on November 3 then deepened on the 4th. This trough brought cooler temperatures to the area, light precipitation on November 2 and a period of gusty winds to the Sierra Nevada and to eastern Kern County between the evening of November 2 and the early morning of November 4. Precipitation amounts were generally very light (a tenth of an inch or less) with a shortwave which moved through the area during the morning of November 2. However, increased gradients produced wind gusts exceeding 45 mph at at several locations between the evening of November 2 and the early morning of November 4. There were some stations that had brief gusts in excess of 60 mph. The winds diminished during the early morning of November 4 as gradients decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This rounded out a series of 3 AR fed storm systems that arrived late on the 24th, and continued to the 26th. This third, colder, storm system in the series brought another round of precipitation to the Sierra and western Nevada. Early on, this colder system held the best potential for accumulating snowfall. Blended guidance initially showed a 30-40% chance of 3 feet of snow along the Sierra crest, a 25-35% chance of 12 inches of snow in Sierra communities, like Mammoth and South Lake Tahoe, and only a 10-15% chance of an inch of snow for lower western NV areas by early morning on the 23rd. Although the liquid storm totals with this storm were not nearly as impressive as the previous storm system, the colder air, and lower snow levels, allowed for a potential of higher snow totals. Follow-up projections still anticipated the potential for another foot or more of snow above 7000 feet near the Sierra crest with a greater than 75% chance for Mono/Alpine counties, but with less certainty (30-60% chance) around the Tahoe Basin. For Sierra/Tahoe communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin, there was still about a 25-50% chance for at least 4 inches of snowfall. Western NV valleys looked to have mainly light rain showers, with maybe a brief rain-snow mix toward the end of the event on the evening/night of the 26th. Although breezy periods were projected for this storm system, wind projections were not as strong as the previous storm systems. Wind gusts in the 30-45 mph range were forecasted for valley areas with stronger winds over wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows Ski Base,  and  located 1.0 mi SSW of Northstar Ski Base , CA, reported 24 hour snowfall amounts of  9.0 inches, and 6.0 inches respectively. Storm total snowfall at Northstar Ski Base was reported as 29 inches.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on November 1 and into the Pacific Northwest on November 2 then dropped into the Great Basin on November 3 then deepened on the 4th. This trough brought cooler temperatures to the area, light precipitation on November 2 and a period of gusty winds to the Sierra Nevada and to eastern Kern County between the evening of November 2 and the early morning of November 4. Precipitation amounts were generally very light (a tenth of an inch or less) with a shortwave which moved through the area during the morning of November 2. However, increased gradients produced wind gusts exceeding 45 mph at at several locations between the evening of November 2 and the early morning of November 4. There were some stations that had brief gusts in excess of 60 mph. The winds diminished during the early morning of November 4 as gradients decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system situated off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled a deep moisture plume into central California during the evening of November 22. This moisture surge brought periods of moderate to locally heavy precipitation through the morning of November 23. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between 0.75 and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation which much of the San Joaquin Valley from  Fresno northward picked up between 0.5 and 0.75 inches of rainfall. Precipitation amounts were lower further southward with much of the San Joaquin Valley south of Fresno southward to Delano picked up between 0.1 and 0.5 inches of rainfall and the Sierra Nevada in Tulare County picked up between 0.25 and 0.5 inches of liquid precipitation. Kern County generally picked up less than a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall from this system although it did produce some increased wind gusts at downslope favored locations and a few stations reported gusts exceeding 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This rounded out a series of 3 AR fed storm systems that arrived late on the 24th, and continued to the 26th. This third, colder, storm system in the series brought another round of precipitation to the Sierra and western Nevada. Early on, this colder system held the best potential for accumulating snowfall. Blended guidance initially showed a 30-40% chance of 3 feet of snow along the Sierra crest, a 25-35% chance of 12 inches of snow in Sierra communities, like Mammoth and South Lake Tahoe, and only a 10-15% chance of an inch of snow for lower western NV areas by early morning on the 23rd. Although the liquid storm totals with this storm were not nearly as impressive as the previous storm system, the colder air, and lower snow levels, allowed for a potential of higher snow totals. Follow-up projections still anticipated the potential for another foot or more of snow above 7000 feet near the Sierra crest with a greater than 75% chance for Mono/Alpine counties, but with less certainty (30-60% chance) around the Tahoe Basin. For Sierra/Tahoe communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin, there was still about a 25-50% chance for at least 4 inches of snowfall. Western NV valleys looked to have mainly light rain showers, with maybe a brief rain-snow mix toward the end of the event on the evening/night of the 26th. Although breezy periods were projected for this storm system, wind projections were not as strong as the previous storm systems. Wind gusts in the 30-45 mph range were forecasted for valley areas with stronger winds over wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base reported, and a trained spotter located 1.0 mi SW of Toms Place, CA, reported a 24 hour snowfall totals of 23.0 inches and 10.6 inches respectively. Storm total snowfall at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base was reported as 30.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly clear skies prevailed across the area during the evening of December 6 as high pressure remained over central California. With winds remaining light across the San Joaquin Valley along with the presence of low level moisture from recent rainfall and inversion conditions, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 6 along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise on December 7. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Visalia. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Visalia and Tulare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of precipitation brought periods of heavy rain, mountain snow and gusty winds to interior Northern California over the Christmas holiday and through the end of the month. Storm total snow amounts of 10 to 40 inches were measured in the northern Sierra Nevada and southern Cascades from December 24th through 29th that led to periods of chain restrictions along the mountain passes. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed during the evening of November 5 in between a strong upper low pressure system centered near the Four Corners area and a large high pressure ridge off the California coast. The strong gradient produced a period of gusty offshore winds over southern and eastern Kern County on November 6 with several stations the the Tehachapi and Grapevine areas as well as across the Mojave Deserts slopes and the desert areas measuring gusts in excess of 45 mph. The winds decreased during the morning of November 7 as gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station 5NW Lebec measures a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure center moved eastward across northern California on November 11. A cold front that was associated with the upper low pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening producing widespread wind gusts above 45 mph as well as areas of blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero in the San Joaquin Valley. The dust resulted in a 20 vehicle accident near Chowchilla which led to SR 152 being closed for much of the afternoon. Many stations measured between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with this system with several stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet with several stations in the higher Sierra Nevada picking up between 2 and 5 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation ended during the evening of November 11 and winds diminished during the early morning of November 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a 20 vehicle collision which led to several injuries on SR 152 at the intersection of Road 17 near Chowchilla as a dust storm pushed through the area. The Merced and Madera ASOS's both had visibility drop to 1.5 to 1.75 miles and had gusts between 40 and 50 mph. The portion of SR 152 south of Chowchilla was closed for a portion of the afternoon due to the collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system situated off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled a deep moisture plume into central California during the evening of November 22. This moisture surge brought periods of moderate to locally heavy precipitation through the morning of November 23. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between 0.75 and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation which much of the San Joaquin Valley from  Fresno northward picked up between 0.5 and 0.75 inches of rainfall. Precipitation amounts were lower further southward with much of the San Joaquin Valley south of Fresno southward to Delano picked up between 0.1 and 0.5 inches of rainfall and the Sierra Nevada in Tulare County picked up between 0.25 and 0.5 inches of liquid precipitation. Kern County generally picked up less than a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall from this system although it did produce some increased wind gusts at downslope favored locations and a few stations reported gusts exceeding 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kaiser Point (9131 ft) picked up an estimated 13 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out some in portions of the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of November 23 which allowed for some areas of dense fog to form in the valley between the late evening of November 23 and the morning of November 24 with a few locations reporting visibility reduced to below an eighth of a mile. This impacted travel along SR 99 for a few hours during the morning of November 24 between north Fresno and Chowchilla. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Porterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure strengthened over California on December 9 resulting in the continuation of mainly clear skies, light winds and inversion conditions over the San Joaquin Valley. As the high was strengthening during the evening of December 8, areas of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley along portions of the SR 99 and SR 198 corridors which reduced visibility to an eighth of a mile. The fog expanded overnight and prevailed over much of the center of the San Joaquin Valley by sunrise on December 9. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Visalia and Porterville. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Visalia, Tulare and Porterville. Several school districts either opened late or delayed or cancelled bus service due to low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large high pressure ridge which had built into California on December 9 resulted in warmer temperatures and drier air over the lower elevations during the afternoon of December 9. This resulted in fog forming later and less prevalent in coverage in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 10 although portions of the SR 99 corridor were still impacted. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Merced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system which had remained nearly stationary off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days moved inland across the Pacific Northwest on November 26. This low pulled up a surge of deep moisture into central California between the evening of November 25 and the evening of November 26. This atmospheric river type system brought moderate to locally heavy precipitation to much of the area with most of the San Joaquin Valley picking up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of rainfall. Most of the Sierra Foothills and the Kern River Valley picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 7000 feet picking up an estimated 1 to 3 feet of new snow. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing limited precipitation in the Kern County Desert areas. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement in Fresno, Tulare and Kern Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported approximately 10 boulders on both lanes of SR 178 4SW Lake Isabella.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed on the 20th with the approach of an upper level storm system that hovered off the Pacific NW. This allowed for a deep plume of moisture, aka atmospheric river (AR), to be advected towards northern CA for the second half of the week. This enhanced the primary hazard being heavy precipitation across the region with projected liquid storm totals of 2.5-3.0 inches for the Sierra crest, 0.75-1.00 inch for the foothills, and 0.10-0.75 from the Basin and Range into far western Nevada. The secondary hazard that was expected with this system was gusty southwest to south winds. Projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the valleys across the region were complemented with gusts exceeding 70-80 mph across the Sierra crest. Blended guidance showed a 30-50% chance of 50+ mph gusts, while ECMWF Extreme Forecast Index (EFI) also indicated an anomalous event. Other wind impacts included turbulence, hazardous boating conditions, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  trained spotter located 5.0 mi WNW of Susanville, CA, and a trained spotter located 6.0 mi SSE of Susanville, CA, reported total rainfall amounts of 1.23 inches and 2.90 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second wave brought higher snow levels with heavy rain and high elevation snow. Although significant rises were anticipated on creeks and streams, no river flooding was forecast. This second round featured more widespread precipitation that reached western Nevada and Mono County. This included greater rain and snowfall totals compared to the first round. Snow levels begin above 7500- 8000' at the onset of precipitation as the region remained situated in the warm air regime. This setup, once again confined snowfall to the High Sierra with rainfall elsewhere. By early on the 23rd a transition into the colder side of the storm lowered projected snow levels down to 6000-7000' that marked the beginning of snowfall down to Sierra passes with travel impacts as well. A foot or two of snow was projected along the Sierra crest during this second round with a 20% chance of highest peaks receiving 3 feet or more of snowfall. Meanwhile, only an inch or less was projected for Sierra communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin. Ensemble simulations also leaned toward a stronger burst of winds ahead of the precipitation. Blended guidance highlighted a 40-50% chance for wind gusts exceeding 50 mph for the afternoon and evening of the 22nd along the Highway 395, and Interstate 580 corridor. These conditions brought impacts to backcountry recreation, hazardous lake conditions, high profile vehicle travel restrictions on area roadways, and aviation turbulence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.5 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain, a mesonet station (XMLC1-Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, and a mesonet station (565SE Sce-3577) located  4.0 mi NNE of Twin Lakes, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph, 62 mph, and 60 mph respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations reported morning low temperatures of 32 to 37 degrees at elevations below 2000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second wave brought higher snow levels with heavy rain and high elevation snow. Although significant rises were anticipated on creeks and streams, no river flooding was forecast. This second round featured more widespread precipitation that reached western Nevada and Mono County. This included greater rain and snowfall totals compared to the first round. Snow levels begin above 7500- 8000' at the onset of precipitation as the region remained situated in the warm air regime. This setup, once again confined snowfall to the High Sierra with rainfall elsewhere. By early on the 23rd a transition into the colder side of the storm lowered projected snow levels down to 6000-7000' that marked the beginning of snowfall down to Sierra passes with travel impacts as well. A foot or two of snow was projected along the Sierra crest during this second round with a 20% chance of highest peaks receiving 3 feet or more of snowfall. Meanwhile, only an inch or less was projected for Sierra communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin. Ensemble simulations also leaned toward a stronger burst of winds ahead of the precipitation. Blended guidance highlighted a 40-50% chance for wind gusts exceeding 50 mph for the afternoon and evening of the 22nd along the Highway 395, and Interstate 580 corridor. These conditions brought impacts to backcountry recreation, hazardous lake conditions, high profile vehicle travel restrictions on area roadways, and aviation turbulence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP Observer located at Tahoe City(TAHC1), CA, reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 1.16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure kept skies mainly clear and winds light across the San Joaquin Vallely during the morning of November 13.  With recent rains providing for a low level moisture supply and inversion conditions present, some dense fog formed along the SR 198 corridor in Kings County from the SR 43 exchange westward to Lemoore Naval Air Station where visibility was reduced to an eighth of a mile. Elsewhere in the valley, visibility was generally in the one to four mile range. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following and during a record heat wave, 3 large wildfires erupted and became out of control burning rapidly. The Airport fire started in Trabuco Canyon in Orange County on the south portion of the Santa Ana mountains. The Bridge fire started in the San Gabriel mountains and spread into Wrightwood and Mt Baldy. The Line fire started in Highland and burned for weeks up the Santa Ana River drainage of the San Bernardino mountains. The wildfires were between 23,0000 and 55,000 acres and caused millions of dollars of damage and expenses to fight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Airport fire began at 120 pm PDT Monday September 9, 2024 in Trabuco Canyon, at a remote controlled airport facility, following an excessive heat wave where temperatures reached 100 to 112F. The wildfire burned the next 2 weeks across the Santa Ana mountains and SR 74 which was closed. The wildfire reached into western Riverside County, just west of Lake Elsinore. Total acreage was 23,500. Estimated cost of 95 million. There were a total of 160 structures destroyed in Riverside and Orange Counties with 89 of those structures in Orange County. 22 persons, 2 were civilians and the rest firefighters, suffered injuries. A Fire Management Assistance Grant was issued and approved by FEMA through the California.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following and during a record heat wave, 3 large wildfires erupted and became out of control burning rapidly. The Airport fire started in Trabuco Canyon in Orange County on the south portion of the Santa Ana mountains. The Bridge fire started in the San Gabriel mountains and spread into Wrightwood and Mt Baldy. The Line fire started in Highland and burned for weeks up the Santa Ana River drainage of the San Bernardino mountains. The wildfires were between 23,0000 and 55,000 acres and caused millions of dollars of damage and expenses to fight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Airport fire began at 120 pm PDT Monday September 9, 2024 in Trabuco Canyon of Orange County (remote control airport facility) and then burned to the Santa Ana mountain crest and Santiago Peak and reached into western Riverside County on September 10th, 2024. The wildfire caused major damage in the small town of El Cariso village and prompted evacuations into Lake Elsinore. Riverside County reported 55 structures destroyed and 7 homes severe damaged. There were about 50 vehicles that were burned and damaged, which many were historical models and featured in movies.  Over 150 structures were destroyed in including 119 homes due to the Airport wildfire between Orange and Riverside counties. Fire Management Assistance Grant (FMAG) was awarded for the Airport wildfire with total damage estimated at 95 million. Reports indicated that up to 400 million in claims were filed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought heavy mountain snow with mountain travel delays and chain restrictions, widespread rain with flooding impacts, and gusty winds from November 18th through November 21st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave developed starting Labor Day weekend and peaked the next weekend September 5 to 9, 2024. The hottest days included Ramona reaching 114, Santa Ana 113,  and Anaheim an all-time record of 112.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperature reached a record 112 in Anaheim on September 6, 2024.  The heat wave lasted through September 9th with high temperatures of 104 to 113F across inland Orange County. A woman and a child were found in a locked vehicle on September 6th. The child died at the hospital. Authorities reported the air temperature at 104 when they arrived at 420 pm. Authorities reported that alcohol was involved and the mother of the child was later arrested. 112 exceeds the prior all-time high temperature at Anaheim which was 109 set in September 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another pre-winter storm system swings another round of snow into the region. Locally heavy snowfall was forecasted near South Lake Tahoe, Carson City, and areas southeast up to and along the Sierra Front. This brought slick roads and travel delays in the Sierra, and some higher elevations of far western Nevada like Virginia City. This time, snow levels dropped close to valley floors Friday that brought a higher chance for a mix of rain and snow across the Reno-Carson City urban area. This also allowed for the 10-20% chance for up to an inch of snow around foothill areas above 5000feet. This system brought minor impacts to the Friday afternoon commute in valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Central Sierra Snow Lab located 1.0 mi W of Donner, CA, reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 2.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second wave brought higher snow levels with heavy rain and high elevation snow. Although significant rises were anticipated on creeks and streams, no river flooding was forecast. This second round featured more widespread precipitation that reached western Nevada and Mono County. This included greater rain and snowfall totals compared to the first round. Snow levels begin above 7500- 8000' at the onset of precipitation as the region remained situated in the warm air regime. This setup, once again confined snowfall to the High Sierra with rainfall elsewhere. By early on the 23rd a transition into the colder side of the storm lowered projected snow levels down to 6000-7000' that marked the beginning of snowfall down to Sierra passes with travel impacts as well. A foot or two of snow was projected along the Sierra crest during this second round with a 20% chance of highest peaks receiving 3 feet or more of snowfall. Meanwhile, only an inch or less was projected for Sierra communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin. Ensemble simulations also leaned toward a stronger burst of winds ahead of the precipitation. Blended guidance highlighted a 40-50% chance for wind gusts exceeding 50 mph for the afternoon and evening of the 22nd along the Highway 395, and Interstate 580 corridor. These conditions brought impacts to backcountry recreation, hazardous lake conditions, high profile vehicle travel restrictions on area roadways, and aviation turbulence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows summit, CA, a mesonet station (HVRTM-Blue Streak Weather Station) located 1.0 NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA,  and a mesonet station (RNYC1-Rice Canyon) located 5.0 mi SSE of Sierraville, CA reported peak wind gusts of 146 mph, 83 mph, and 76 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following and during a record heat wave, 3 large wildfires erupted and became out of control burning rapidly. The Airport fire started in Trabuco Canyon in Orange County on the south portion of the Santa Ana mountains. The Bridge fire started in the San Gabriel mountains and spread into Wrightwood and Mt Baldy. The Line fire started in Highland and burned for weeks up the Santa Ana River drainage of the San Bernardino mountains. The wildfires were between 23,0000 and 55,000 acres and caused millions of dollars of damage and expenses to fight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Line fire began at 930 am September 5, 2024 at Baseline and Aplin Streets near Highland California.  High temperatures on September 5 were 107 to 112F where the fire began. The fire burned north into the Santa Ana drainage due to upslope wind flow. The wildfire grew to 36,000 acres over the next 2 weeks and was finally contained at 44,000 acres in early October. The fire was difficult to contain due to the activity in the remote and thick Bear Creek drainage. The wildfire burned a 98 year old Lookout tower on Keller Peak. The wildfire did severe damage to SR 330 including miles of guardrails and signs which was closed for repairs until November 24, 2024. The fire was contained at the ridge line at Snow Valley to Bear Mountain along SR 18.  Arson was determined to be the cause of the fire. The wildfire destroyed 5 structures in Running Springs and injured 4 firefighters. Estimated costs were 14.5 million. The Fire Management Assistance Grant (FMAG) was approved. Air quality index reached 1500 in Big Bear City causing the hospital to close and transport patients. The wildfire experienced a large flare up in Bear Creek on September 29th and burned an additional several thousand acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough with embedded shortwave trough digs into the Pacific Northwest in early November. Bringing with it blustery winds and precipitation in various forms resulting in hazards and utility/infrastructure disruption.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to non-thunderstorm wind gusts, power to 110 homes had interuption. This occured 2 miles southeast of Kneeland, CA. Lat 40.73N, Long -123.95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following and during a record heat wave, 3 large wildfires erupted and became out of control burning rapidly. The Airport fire started in Trabuco Canyon in Orange County on the south portion of the Santa Ana mountains. The Bridge fire started in the San Gabriel mountains and spread into Wrightwood and Mt Baldy. The Line fire started in Highland and burned for weeks up the Santa Ana River drainage of the San Bernardino mountains. The wildfires were between 23,0000 and 55,000 acres and caused millions of dollars of damage and expenses to fight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bridge fire began at 215 pm PDT September 8th, 2024, suspected to have started by lightning. Excessive heat occurred leading up to the day of the wildfire.  The wildfire grew to 35,000 acres on Tuesday September 10th, burning along the ridgeline of the San Gabriel mountains up to 8000 feet MSL and destroying 20 homes in Mt Baldy and 3 in Wrightwood during the evening and night of September 9th. The wildfire created wind gusts up to 40 mph allowing it to become out of control in Wrightwood. There was a Red Flag warning in effect for onshore west winds in the San Gabriel mountains of LA County. The wildfire burned up to to 56,000 acres. 81 structures were destroyed. Defensible space was credited for minimizing the damages to homes. Mountain High ski  area recorded the wildfire on September 10th when it burned over the ridge and through the resort. Videos showed the snowmaking (water hydrants) were used to protect structures. 3 resort structures were destroyed along with powerlines but the main lodges were saved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday September 7, a monsoonal easterly wind flow weather pattern set up and thunderstorms developed across the mountains and spread across the Inland Empire. Some of these storms had very heavy rainfall and prolific lightning. The actively burning Line fire was affected and helped develop additional persistent thunderstorms. On Sunday September 8, thunderstorms were most active in the Inland Empire where flash flooding and strong winds occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Socal Edison weather station on Ellis avenue recorded gust to 71 mph.  Wind damage was observed during storm survey on September 9. WCM and new meteorologist surveyed area on September 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms n northeast wind flow erupted September 20, 2024, producing heavy rain, hail and snow at the end. There was also a large funnel cloud observed in the San Jacinto mountains west of Palm Desert. Minor flooding and debris flows occurred on the Line burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several photos and videos of a long lived extended funnel cloud moving north to south along the San Jacinto ridgeline, viewed from La Quinta. Convective showers and thunderstorms occurred over the mountains and a few moved into the valleys. Video showed clear rotation of the funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of mountain snow and wind impacts developed across the Sierra |and western NV as another upper short wave trough, and attendant cold front, |lifted across northern CA late morning on November 13th. This next pre-winter storm system spread light to moderate showers across the region with much of the heavier precipitation staying west of Hwy-395 in Lassen County. Then shower chances increased to the south-southeast into the Lake Tahoe Basin by mid afternoon. This storm system also brought another period of gusty southwest winds to the region as well. Impacts included travel for high profile vehicles on area roadways, increased turbulence for local aviation interests, and elevated fire weather concerns across portions of far western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM-Blue Streak Weather Station) located 1.0  mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit reported a peak wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This next storm system and attendant cold front brought periods of light valley rain and higher elevation snow, gusty winds, along with colder temperatures. The strongest winds from west and northwest occurred overnight on November 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th. Gusts up to 30-40 mph were expected in western Nevada valleys, while Sierra ridges saw winds above 70 mph. The bulk of the rainfall fell across NE California and northern Nevada, with a 70-90% chance for the Sierra crest to accumulate another 1-2 with some terrain favored areas west of Lake Tahoe seeing up to 4 inches of snowfall along the crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.0  mi ENE of Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moving across the Desert Southwest resulted in gusty westerly winds across portions of southeast CA. Imperial County Airport ASOS nearby recorded peak wind gusts ranging between 35 and 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown onto road leading to a road closure of Bennett Road from Even Hewes Highway to Ross Road. Imperial County Airport nearby reported peak wind gusts up to 35-40 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spotty convective showers developed in the morning around Imperial County. These cells were fairly weak and may have produced a lightning flash or two along with some very light rainfall. At least one of the cells near Holtville managed to produce a localized microburst that knocked over around 50 power poles. The environment was favorable for strong downbursts given a dry sub-cloud layer with objective analysis showing DCAPE around 1400-1500 J/kg at the time of the event. Given the loose sandy soils of the area, power poles can fall over with sub-severe wind speeds. Thus, considering this, the environment, and the relatively weak intensity of the convective showers, winds were estimated at 50 to 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 50 power poles were blown down along a north-south oriented dirt road that passes by the Ormesa 2 Geothermal Site east of Holtville. Timing estimated from radar and winds were estimated at 50-55 mph based on radar and the environment. Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In an environment characterized by PWAT values of around 1.5 with MLCAPE values near 1000 J/KG, thunderstorm activity developed across the Mohave Desert during the early afternoon hours. An isolated strong thunderstorm developed over the higher terrain area of Joshua Tree National Park, where it remained anchored and dumped over an inch of rain in less than one hour, enough rainfall in a short time span to cause road closures due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads along the Indian Cove Campground Site in Joshua Tree National Park closed due to flooding. No damages nor injuries were reported.
